# Financial Calculator Formulas & Reference Data

## Core Formulas

### Compound Interest
A = P × (1 + r/n)^(n×t)
- A = final amount
- P = principal (starting amount)
- r = annual interest rate (decimal)
- n = number of times compounded per year (12 for monthly)
- t = number of years

Example: $10,000 at 7% compounded monthly for 20 years
A = 10000 × (1 + 0.07/12)^(12×20) = $40,387.39

### Future Value of Regular Contributions
FV = PMT × [((1 + r/n)^(n×t) - 1) / (r/n)]
- PMT = regular payment amount
- Example: $500/month at 10% for 30 years = $1,130,243

### Debt Payoff Calculator
Months to payoff = -log(1 - (balance × rate/12) / payment) / log(1 + rate/12)
Total interest = (monthly_payment × months) - original_balance

### Mortgage Affordability
Maximum monthly housing payment = Gross monthly income × 0.28
Maximum total debt payments = Gross monthly income × 0.36

### Monthly Mortgage Payment
M = P × [r(1+r)^n] / [(1+r)^n - 1]
- M = monthly payment
- P = loan principal
- r = monthly interest rate (annual rate / 12)
- n = total number of payments (years × 12)

### Emergency Fund Target
Target = Monthly essential expenses × Coverage months
Essential expenses = Rent + Utilities + Food + Insurance + Transportation + Min debt payments

### Retirement Number (4% Rule)
Amount needed = Annual retirement expenses × 25
Example: Need $50,000/year → Need $1,250,000 saved

### Credit Utilization
Utilization = (Total balances / Total credit limits) × 100
Target: Under 30%, ideal under 10%

### Debt-to-Income Ratio
DTI = (Total monthly debt payments / Gross monthly income) × 100
Target: Under 36%, mortgage approval typically needs under 43%

### Investment Growth (Rule of 72)
Years to double = 72 / Annual return rate
At 7% return: 72/7 = ~10.3 years to double
At 10% return: 72/10 = ~7.2 years to double

### Inflation-Adjusted Return
Real return = ((1 + nominal return) / (1 + inflation rate)) - 1
If stocks return 10% and inflation is 3%: real return = 6.8%

### Break-Even on Refinancing
Months to break even = Total closing costs / Monthly payment savings
If closing costs = $4,000 and you save $150/month: break even in 27 months

## Reference Data Tables

### 2025 Federal Tax Brackets (Single Filer)
| Taxable Income | Rate |
|---------------|------|
| $0 - $11,600 | 10% |
| $11,601 - $47,150 | 12% |
| $47,151 - $100,525 | 22% |
| $100,526 - $191,950 | 24% |
| $191,951 - $243,725 | 32% |
| $243,726 - $609,350 | 35% |
| Over $609,350 | 37% |
| Standard Deduction | $15,000 |

### 2025 Federal Tax Brackets (Married Filing Jointly)
| Taxable Income | Rate |
|---------------|------|
| $0 - $23,200 | 10% |
| $23,201 - $94,300 | 12% |
| $94,301 - $201,050 | 22% |
| $201,051 - $383,900 | 24% |
| $383,901 - $487,450 | 32% |
| $487,451 - $731,200 | 35% |
| Over $731,200 | 37% |
| Standard Deduction | $30,000 |

### Average Returns by Asset Class (Historical)
| Asset | Avg Annual Return | Risk Level |
|-------|------------------|-----------|
| S&P 500 | ~10% | Moderate-High |
| Total US Stock Market | ~10% | Moderate-High |
| International Stocks | ~7-8% | Moderate-High |
| US Bonds | ~4-5% | Low-Moderate |
| Real Estate (REITs) | ~8-10% | Moderate |
| High-Yield Savings | ~4-5% (current) | Very Low |
| CDs | ~4-5% (current) | Very Low |
| Bitcoin (10yr) | ~50%+ (extremely volatile) | Very High |
| Inflation (historical avg) | ~3% | N/A |

### Average Monthly Expenses (US Household)
| Category | Average | % of Income |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Housing | $1,885 | 33% |
| Transportation | $1,025 | 16% |
| Food | $810 | 13% |
| Insurance/Pensions | $775 | 12% |
| Healthcare | $425 | 8% |

| Clothing | $155 | 2% |
| Education | $115 | 2% |
| Other | $550 | 9% |
Source: BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey

### Contribution Limits (2025)
| Account | Limit | Catch-up (50+) |
|---------|-------|---------------|
| 401(k) / 403(b) | $23,500 | +$7,500 |
| Traditional/Roth IRA | $7,000 | +$1,000 |
| HSA (Single) | $4,300 | +$1,000 |
| HSA (Family) | $8,550 | +$1,000 |
| 529 Plan | No federal limit | N/A |
| FSA | $3,300 | N/A |
| Dependent Care FSA | $5,000 | N/A |

### Key Financial Benchmarks by Age
| Age | Savings Target | Retirement Multiple |
|-----|---------------|-------------------|
| 25 | $10,000+ emergency fund | 0.5x salary saved |
| 30 | 3-6 months expenses | 1x salary |
| 35 | 6 months expenses | 2x salary |
| 40 | 6 months expenses | 3x salary |
| 45 | 6 months expenses | 4x salary |
| 50 | 6-9 months expenses | 6x salary |
| 55 | 9 months expenses | 7x salary |
| 60 | 12 months expenses | 8x salary |
| 65 | 12 months expenses | 10x salary |
Source: Fidelity Investments guidelines

# Budgeting Methods

## The 50/30/20 Rule
Split after-tax (take-home) income into three categories:
- **50% Needs:** Housing, groceries, utilities, insurance, minimum debt payments, transportation
- **30% Wants:** Dining out, entertainment, subscriptions, shopping, travel, hobbies
- **20% Savings & Debt Payoff:** Emergency fund, retirement contributions, extra debt payments, investments

### Example on $5,000/month take-home:
- Needs: $2,500
- Wants: $1,500
- Savings: $1,000

If your needs exceed 50%, focus on reducing your largest fixed expenses (housing and transportation).

## Zero-Based Budget
Every dollar gets assigned a job. Income minus all categories equals exactly zero.
- Best for people who want detailed control
- Can use apps like YNAB (You Need A Budget) or EveryDollar

## Pay Yourself First
1. Set up automatic transfers on payday to savings and investment accounts
2. Pay all bills
3. Whatever is left is your spending money
- Best for people who hate tracking every purchase

## Envelope Method
- Withdraw cash for variable spending categories
- Put cash in physical (or digital) envelopes: Groceries, Dining, Entertainment, etc.
- When an envelope is empty, stop spending in that category
- Best for overspenders who need tangible limits

## Common Budget Mistakes
1. **Not tracking subscriptions** — Average American spends $219/month on subscriptions
2. **Forgetting irregular expenses** — Car maintenance, gifts, annual fees
3. **Being too restrictive** — Budgets without fun money fail. Include a guilt-free category.
4. **Not adjusting** — Review and adjust monthly for the first 3 months

## Budget Percentages by Category (Recommended)
- Housing: 25-30%
- Transportation: 10-15%
- Food (groceries + dining): 10-15%
- Insurance: 10-15%
- Savings/Investments: 15-20%
- Utilities: 5-10%
- Entertainment: 5-10%
- Personal/Misc: 5-10%

## High-Yield Savings for Budget Goals
Keep your emergency fund and short-term savings in a high-yield savings account earning 4-5% APY (2025 rates). Top options: Marcus by Goldman Sachs, Ally Bank, Wealthfront, SoFi.
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/credit_scores.md  (59 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Credit Scores and Reports

## Understanding Your Credit Score

### FICO Score Ranges (300-850)
| Score | Rating | Population % | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 800-850 | Exceptional | ~21% | Best rates on everything |
| 740-799 | Very Good | ~25% | Near-best rates |
| 670-739 | Good | ~21% | Average rates |
| 580-669 | Fair | ~17% | Subprime rates, higher costs |
| 300-579 | Poor | ~16% | May be denied credit |

### The 5 Factors
1. **Payment History (35%)** — Most important. Even one 30-day late payment can drop your score 60-110 points. Set up autopay for minimums on everything.
2. **Credit Utilization (30%)** — Balance ÷ credit limit. Keep under 30%, ideally under 10%. Applies to each card AND overall. Pay before statement closing date to lower reported utilization.
3. **Length of Credit History (15%)** — Average age of all accounts. Never close your oldest credit card. If you have no credit history, become an authorized user on a family member's old card.
4. **Credit Mix (10%)** — Having different types (credit card, auto loan, mortgage, student loan) helps slightly.
5. **New Inquiries (10%)** — Each hard inquiry costs 5-10 points temporarily. Multiple inquiries for same type (mortgage shopping) within 14-45 days count as one.

## Building Credit from Scratch

### Step 1: Get Your First Credit Card
- Secured credit card (requires deposit): Discover It Secured, Capital One Platinum Secured
- Student credit card if in college
- Become authorized user on parent's/spouse's card with good history

### Step 2: Use It Responsibly
- Use for ONE small recurring purchase (gas, streaming subscription)
- Pay balance in FULL every month (before the due date)
- Never use more than 30% of your limit

### Step 3: Wait and Build
- After 6 months, you'll have a scoreable credit file
- After 12-24 months, apply for a better card

## Quick Ways to Improve Your Score
1. **Pay down credit cards** to under 30% utilization (fastest impact: 1-2 months)
2. **Get added as authorized user** on someone's old, low-utilization card
3. **Dispute errors** on credit report (30% of reports contain errors)
4. **Request credit limit increase** (lowers utilization ratio without paying anything)
5. **Keep old accounts open** even if you don't use them
6. **Use Experian Boost** (free, adds utility/phone payment history)

## Checking Your Credit
- **Free credit reports:** AnnualCreditReport.com (free weekly from all 3 bureaus)
- **Free credit scores:** Credit Karma, Discover, many bank apps
- Checking your own credit is a "soft inquiry" — it never hurts your score

## The Cost of Bad Credit
On a $300,000 30-year mortgage:
- 6.5% rate (excellent credit): $1,896/month — $382,633 total interest
- 8.0% rate (poor credit): $2,201/month — $492,467 total interest
- **Difference: $109,834** more in interest over the life of the loan

## Protecting Your Credit
- Freeze credit at all 3 bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) — free, prevents unauthorized accounts
- Monitor for suspicious activity monthly
- Never share credit card numbers over unsecured channels
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/debt_management.md  (63 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Debt Management

## Types of Debt

### Good Debt (Low Interest, Builds Value)
- Mortgage (typically 6-7.5% in 2025)
- Student loans (federal: 5.5% for undergrad, 7% for grad in 2025)
- Business loans for proven concepts

### Bad Debt (High Interest, No Asset)
- Credit cards (average APR: 22-28% in 2025)
- Payday loans (300-500%+ APR — avoid at all costs)
- Personal loans for consumption (8-36% APR)
- Buy Now Pay Later with missed payments

## Debt Payoff Strategies

### The Avalanche Method (Mathematically Optimal)
Pay minimum on everything, throw all extra at the HIGHEST interest rate debt.
- Saves the most money in total interest
- Can feel slow if your highest-rate debt is also your largest balance
- Best for disciplined, numbers-driven people

### The Snowball Method (Psychologically Powerful)
Pay minimum on everything, throw all extra at the SMALLEST balance debt.
- Quick wins build motivation
- You may pay more total interest
- 70% completion rate vs ~50% for avalanche
- Best for people who need motivation wins

### Debt Consolidation
Combine multiple debts into one payment, ideally at a lower interest rate.
- Balance transfer cards: 0% APR for 12-21 months (3-5% transfer fee)
- Personal consolidation loan: Usually 8-15% APR
- Only works if you DON'T rack up new debt on the freed-up cards

## Debt Priority Ladder
1. Make minimum payments on ALL debts (always)
2. Get employer 401(k) match (free money)
3. Pay off high-interest debt (>7% APR) aggressively
4. Build emergency fund to 3-6 months
5. Pay medium-interest debt (5-7%) OR invest (roughly equal)
6. Low-interest debt (< 5%) can coexist with investing

## Key Numbers
- Total U.S. household debt (2025): $17.9 trillion
- Average credit card balance: $6,501
- Average student loan balance: $37,338
- Average car loan: $23,792

## Debt-to-Income Ratio
- Monthly debt payments ÷ gross monthly income × 100
- Under 20%: Healthy
- 20-35%: Manageable
- 36-49%: Struggling
- 50%+: Crisis — seek help

## When to Seek Professional Help
- Debt-to-income ratio over 50%
- Considering bankruptcy
- Being sued by creditors
- Unable to make minimum payments
Contact: National Foundation for Credit Counseling (NFCC.org) for free or low-cost counseling.
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/emergency_funds.md  (53 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Emergency Funds

## What Is an Emergency Fund?
Cash reserves set aside for unexpected expenses — job loss, medical bills, car repairs, home emergencies. This is your financial safety net.

## How Much Do You Need?

### The Standard Rule: 3-6 Months of Essential Expenses
Essential expenses include: rent/mortgage, groceries, utilities, insurance, transportation, minimum debt payments.

### Your Number Depends On:
- **3 months** if: Dual income household, stable job, no dependents
- **6 months** if: Single income, variable income, have dependents, self-employed
- **9-12 months** if: Freelancer, seasonal worker, commission-based income, single parent

### Quick Calculation
Monthly essentials × months of coverage = emergency fund target.
Example: $3,000/month × 6 months = $18,000 target.

## Building Your Emergency Fund

### Starting from Zero
1. **First milestone: $1,000** — Covers most small emergencies (car repair, medical copay, appliance replacement)
2. **Second milestone: 1 month of expenses** — Buys time if income drops
3. **Third milestone: 3 months** — Handles job loss in strong job market
4. **Final target: 6 months** — Full financial security

### Strategies to Build Faster
- **Automate first:** Set up auto-transfer on payday, even $25/week
- **Sell unused items:** Declutter and sell on Facebook Marketplace, eBay
- **Side hustle:** Dedicate 100% of side income to emergency fund until funded
- **Tax refund:** Direct your refund straight to savings
- **Round up:** Use apps like Acorns or bank round-up features

## Where to Keep Your Emergency Fund
- **High-Yield Savings Account (HYSA):** Best option. Earns 4-5% APY (2025), FDIC insured, accessible in 1-2 days.
- **Money Market Account:** Similar yields, may come with check-writing ability.
- **NOT stocks or investments:** Emergency fund must be stable and liquid. Markets can drop 30%+ when you need the money most (recessions often cause both job loss AND market crashes).
- **NOT your checking account:** Too tempting to spend. Keep it separate.

## When to Use Your Emergency Fund
**YES:** Job loss, medical emergency, essential car/home repair, urgent travel for family emergency.
**NO:** Sales, vacations, "good deals," planned expenses, wants.

## After Using Your Emergency Fund
Replenish it as your #1 financial priority before resuming extra investing or discretionary spending.

## Emergency Fund vs. Other Savings
| Type | Purpose | Where to Keep | How Much |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emergency Fund | Unexpected crises | HYSA | 3-6 months expenses |
| Sinking Fund | Planned irregular expenses | HYSA | Varies by goal |
| Investment Account | Long-term wealth | Brokerage | As much as possible |
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/insurance_essentials.md  (61 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Insurance Essentials

## Why Insurance Matters
Insurance protects you from financial catastrophe. The rule: insure against things that would **financially devastate** you. Don't insure small, affordable losses.

## Types of Insurance Everyone Needs

### Health Insurance
- **Average premium (2025):** $477/month individual (employer-sponsored: ~$138 after employer contribution)
- **Key terms:** Premium (monthly cost), deductible (you pay first), copay (fixed per visit), out-of-pocket max (your annual ceiling)
- **HSA-eligible plans (HDHP):** Higher deductible, lower premium, can contribute to HSA for triple tax benefit
- **ACA Marketplace:** Open enrollment Nov 1 – Jan 15. Subsidies available based on income.

### Auto Insurance
- **Required:** Liability coverage (minimum varies by state)
- **Recommended:** Comprehensive + collision if car worth > $10,000
- **Ways to save:** Raise deductible to $1,000, bundle with home insurance, maintain good credit, shop around yearly
- **Average cost (2025):** $2,150/year for full coverage

### Renters Insurance
- **Average cost:** $15-25/month
- **Covers:** Personal belongings (theft, fire, water damage), liability if someone is injured in your apartment
- **Often overlooked** but incredibly valuable. Your landlord's insurance does NOT cover your stuff.

### Homeowners Insurance
- **Average cost (2025):** $2,100/year
- **Covers:** Structure, personal property, liability, additional living expenses
- **Tips:** Review annually, increase coverage as home value rises, document belongings with photos/video
- **Not covered:** Floods (need separate NFIP policy), earthquakes (separate policy in affected areas)

### Life Insurance
- **Who needs it:** Anyone with dependents (spouse, kids, aging parents you support)
- **Type to buy:** Term life insurance (20-30 year term, pure coverage, cheap)
- **How much:** 10-15× your annual income
- **Example:** 30-year-old, healthy, $500K 20-year term: ~$25-35/month
- **Skip:** Whole life insurance (expensive, bad investment returns — invest the savings difference instead)

### Disability Insurance
- **Most overlooked insurance.** You're more likely to become disabled than die before 65.
- **Short-term disability:** Covers 60-70% of income for 3-6 months
- **Long-term disability:** Covers 50-60% of income for years/decades
- **Check your employer benefits first** — many include basic coverage

### Umbrella Insurance
- **Extra liability coverage** beyond auto and home limits
- **Who needs it:** Net worth over $300,000, rental property owners, anyone at risk of lawsuit
- **Cost:** ~$150-300/year for $1 million coverage — incredible value

## Insurance Rules of Thumb
1. **Raise deductibles** to lower premiums (but keep enough cash for the deductible)
2. **Bundle policies** for 10-25% discount
3. **Shop around every 2-3 years** — loyalty penalties are real
4. **Never insure what you can afford to replace** (skip extended warranties, phone insurance)
5. **Review coverage annually** as life changes (marriage, kids, home purchase)

## Insurance Scams to Avoid
- Whole life insurance sold as an "investment"
- Credit card protection insurance
- Extended warranties on electronics
- Single-disease policies (cancer insurance, etc.)
- Mortgage life insurance (get term life instead, it's cheaper and more flexible)
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/investing_basics.md  (51 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Investing Basics

## What Is Investing?
Investing means putting your money to work so it grows over time. Instead of keeping cash under your mattress (where inflation eats its value), you buy assets — stocks, bonds, real estate, or funds — that historically increase in value.

## Key Concepts

### Stocks
A stock is a tiny ownership stake in a company. When the company profits, your share becomes worth more. Historically, the U.S. stock market returns about 10% per year on average (before inflation). Individual years can range from -37% to +52%.

### Bonds
A bond is a loan you make to a government or corporation. They pay you fixed interest and return your principal at maturity. Bonds are less volatile than stocks but offer lower returns (typically 4-6%).

### Index Funds
An index fund tracks an entire market. The S&P 500 index fund owns all 500 of the largest U.S. companies. Index funds charge very low fees (0.03-0.20%) and outperform 90% of professional fund managers over 15+ years.

### ETFs vs Mutual Funds
ETFs trade like stocks throughout the day. Mutual funds trade once per day at market close. ETFs typically have lower expense ratios and better tax efficiency. Both are excellent vehicles for diversified investing.

## Getting Started

### 1. Open a Brokerage Account
Top brokerages for beginners: Fidelity, Charles Schwab, Vanguard. All offer zero-commission trades and no account minimums.

### 2. Start with Index Funds
- **VTI** or **VTSAX** — Total U.S. stock market
- **VOO** or **FXAIX** — S&P 500
- **VXUS** — International stocks
- **BND** — Total bond market

### 3. Dollar-Cost Average
Invest the same amount on a regular schedule regardless of market conditions. This removes emotion and reduces timing risk.

### 4. Diversify
Spread investments across asset classes, sectors, and geographies. A simple 3-fund portfolio (U.S. stocks + international stocks + bonds) covers the entire global market.

## Risk and Return
Higher potential returns always come with higher risk. Stocks return more than bonds but are more volatile. Your risk tolerance depends on your time horizon — money needed in 1-3 years belongs in a savings account, not the stock market.

## Compound Growth
$10,000 invested at 10% annual return:
- Year 10: $25,937
- Year 20: $67,275
- Year 30: $174,494

Adding $500/month:
- Year 10: $127,809
- Year 20: $386,968
- Year 30: $1,085,661

Time is the most powerful factor. Starting 10 years earlier matters more than doubling your monthly contribution.
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/kids_finance.md  (99 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Financial Literacy for Kids and Teens

## Ages 3-5: Money Is Real

### Key Lessons
- Coins and bills have different values
- Things cost money — you can't just take things from stores
- Sometimes you have to wait to get what you want (delayed gratification)

### Activities
- Play store with real coins
- Let them hand money to cashier
- "Do you want this small toy now, or save for the bigger one?"

## Ages 6-10: Earning, Saving, Spending

### Key Lessons
- Money comes from work (not ATMs or phones)
- Three jars: Save, Spend, Share
- Needs vs Wants (food = need, toy = want)
- Setting savings goals

### Activities
- Earn money through age-appropriate chores
- Use clear jars so they SEE money growing
- Match their savings (Bank of Mom/Dad pays interest)
- Let them make spending mistakes with their own money — priceless learning

### Allowance Tips
- Tie some money to chores, give some unconditionally
- Start with age-appropriate amount ($0.50-$1 per year of age per week)
- Don't bail them out when they spend it all early

## Ages 11-14: Compound Interest and Entrepreneurship

### Key Lessons
- How compound interest works (use concrete examples)
- Basics of budgeting (income - expenses = savings)
- How banks work
- Inflation: why $10 today ≠ $10 in 20 years
- Introduction to entrepreneurship

### Activities
- Open a youth savings account (many banks allow with parent)
- Teach them to track spending for one month
- Start a micro-business: lawn care, pet sitting, crafts, tutoring
- Play stock market simulator games
- Show them YOUR budget (age-appropriate transparency)

### The Compound Interest Story
If a 12-year-old invests $50/month starting at 15 until age 65:
- Total invested: $30,000
- Value at 65 (8% return): ~$349,000
If they wait until 25: $30,000 invested → ~$149,000 (less than half!)

## Ages 15-18: Real World Prep

### Key Lessons
- How taxes work (they'll see it on first paycheck!)
- Credit and credit scores (NOT debt = normal)
- Compound interest on debt (credit cards)
- Basic investing (index funds, not day trading)
- Needs vs wants at a higher level (new car vs used)
- How student loans work

### Activities
- Get a part-time job
- Open a Roth IRA (if they have earned income) — best head start ever
- Get a secured credit card and learn responsible use
- Review college cost calculators
- Practice negotiating (cell phone bill, subscription prices)
- Create a personal budget

### First Paycheck Lesson
Show them the difference between gross and net pay. Explain each deduction:
- Federal income tax
- State income tax (if applicable)
- Social Security (6.2%)
- Medicare (1.45%)

## Money Conversations to Have at Every Age
1. "In our family, we choose to spend on X because Y" (values-based spending)
2. "We can't afford that right now" → Better: "That's not in our plan right now"
3. "What do you think that costs?" (develop price awareness)
4. "How could you earn money for that?" (entrepreneurial thinking)
5. "What would happen if you saved that instead?" (opportunity cost)

## Common Mistakes Parents Make
1. Never talking about money (kids learn silence = shame)
2. Always saying yes (kids don't learn delayed gratification)
3. Not letting kids fail with money (small failures now prevent big failures later)
4. Teaching fear of money instead of respect for it
5. Not modeling good behavior (kids watch what you DO, not what you say)

## Resources for Kids
- Books: "The Berenstain Bears' Trouble with Money", "If You Made a Million"
- Games: Monopoly, The Game of Life, Cashflow for Kids
- Apps: Greenlight (kid debit card with savings features), FamZoo, GoHenry
- Agent Richy's Kids Education module with video lessons and quizzes!
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/real_estate.md  (86 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Real Estate and Mortgages

## Home Buying Basics

### The 28/36 Rule
- **28% rule:** Monthly housing costs (mortgage + tax + insurance) should be ≤ 28% of gross monthly income
- **36% rule:** Total debt payments should be ≤ 36% of gross monthly income
- Example on $100K salary ($8,333/month): Max housing = $2,333, max total debt = $3,000

### Down Payment
- **Conventional loan:** 5-20% down. Under 20% requires PMI ($100-300/month on $300K home)
- **FHA loan:** 3.5% down (lower credit score OK, 580+)
- **VA loan:** 0% down (veterans/active military)
- **USDA loan:** 0% down (rural areas, income limits)
- **Saving 20% down on $350K home = $70,000** — takes average buyer 5-7 years

### Current Mortgage Rates (2025)
- 30-year fixed: approximately 6.5-7.5%
- 15-year fixed: approximately 5.5-6.5%
- ARM (adjustable): Lower initial rate, adjusts after 5-7 years

### Closing Costs
- Typically 2-5% of purchase price
- On $350K home: $7,000-17,500
- Includes: appraisal, title insurance, attorney fees, origination fees, prepaid taxes and insurance

## Mortgage Math

### Monthly Payment (30-Year Fixed)
At 7% rate:
| Home Price | Down 20% | Monthly P&I | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $250,000 | $50,000 | $1,331 | $479,017 |
| $350,000 | $70,000 | $1,863 | $670,824 |
| $450,000 | $90,000 | $2,395 | $862,631 |
| $550,000 | $110,000 | $2,928 | $1,054,437 |

Add 30-40% for taxes, insurance, maintenance for true monthly cost.

### 15-Year vs 30-Year
$300K loan at 7% (30-year) vs 6.5% (15-year):
- 30-year: $1,996/month, $418,527 total interest
- 15-year: $2,613/month, $170,362 total interest
- **Savings: $248,165** — but $617/month more

### Refinancing
Worth considering when you can drop rate by 0.75-1%+ and plan to stay 3+ years. Break-even: closing costs ÷ monthly savings = months to recoup.

## Rent vs Buy Decision

### Price-to-Rent Ratio
Home price ÷ annual rent
- Under 15: Buying favored
- 15-20: Roughly equal
- Over 20: Renting may be better

### The 5-Year Rule
Plan to stay at least 5 years to make buying worthwhile after transaction costs (buying costs + selling costs = ~10% of home value).

### Hidden Costs of Ownership
- Property taxes: 0.5-2.5% of home value annually
- Maintenance: 1-2% of home value annually
- Homeowners insurance: $1,500-3,000/year
- HOA fees: $0-$500+/month
- Capital improvements: New roof ($10-25K), HVAC ($5-10K), water heater ($1-3K)

## Real Estate as Investment

### Primary Residence
- Builds equity over time
- Tax benefits: Mortgage interest deduction (if itemizing), up to $250K ($500K married) capital gains exclusion
- Historical appreciation: ~3-5% annually (varies widely by area)
- NOT purely an investment — it's a place to live that may appreciate

### Rental Properties
- Cap rate (net operating income ÷ purchase price) should be > 5%
- Budget for vacancies (8-10% of rental income), maintenance, property management
- Leverage amplifies returns but also risk
- Not passive income without a property manager

## First-Time Buyer Tips
1. Get pre-approved BEFORE house hunting
2. Don't skip the home inspection ($300-500, can save you $50,000+)
3. Budget for unexpected repairs (set aside 1% of home value/year day one)
4. Don't buy the most expensive home you qualify for — that's the bank's max, not yours
5. Consider total commute cost — a cheaper house 30 miles out may cost more in gas and time
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/retirement_planning.md  (58 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Retirement Planning

## How Much Do You Need?

### The 25x Rule
Multiply your desired annual retirement spending by 25. This is based on the 4% safe withdrawal rate.
- $40,000/year lifestyle → $1,000,000 needed
- $60,000/year lifestyle → $1,500,000 needed
- $80,000/year lifestyle → $2,000,000 needed
- $100,000/year lifestyle → $2,500,000 needed

### The 4% Rule
You can withdraw 4% of your portfolio in year one of retirement, then adjust for inflation each year. Historically, this has sustained portfolios for 30+ years in 95% of scenarios.

## Retirement Accounts (2025 Limits)

### 401(k)
- **Employee contribution limit:** $23,500/year
- **Catch-up (age 50+):** additional $7,500 ($31,000 total)
- **Super catch-up (ages 60-63):** additional $11,250 ($34,750 total) — new for 2025
- **Total 401(k) limit (including employer):** $70,000
- Traditional 401(k): pre-tax, reduces taxable income now
- Roth 401(k): after-tax, withdrawals are tax-free in retirement

### IRA (Individual Retirement Account)
- **Contribution limit:** $7,000/year ($8,000 if 50+)
- **Roth IRA income limits:** Phase-out begins at $150,000 (single) / $236,000 (married filing jointly)
- **Traditional IRA:** Deductible if no workplace plan or income below limits
- **Backdoor Roth:** High earners can convert traditional IRA contributions to Roth

### HSA (Health Savings Account)
- **2025 limits:** $4,300 (individual) / $8,550 (family)
- Triple tax advantage: deductible going in, grows tax-free, withdrawals tax-free for medical expenses
- After 65, can withdraw for any purpose (taxed like traditional IRA)
- Best strategy: pay medical expenses out of pocket now, let HSA grow, withdraw tax-free later

## Savings Timeline

### Starting at Age 25 (Target: $1.5M by 65)
- Need ~$350/month at 8% average return
- Total contributed: $168,000 → Grows to $1,500,000

### Starting at Age 35
- Need ~$800/month at 8%
- Total contributed: $288,000 → Grows to $1,500,000

### Starting at Age 45
- Need ~$2,000/month at 8%
- Total contributed: $480,000 → Grows to $1,500,000

The cost of waiting 10 years: you contribute MORE money and end up with LESS. Start now.

## Social Security
- Full retirement age: 67 (for those born 1960+)
- Early: age 62 (reduced ~30%)
- Delayed: up to age 70 (increased ~24%)
- Average monthly benefit (2025): ~$1,976
- Do NOT rely on Social Security alone — it's designed to replace only about 40% of pre-retirement income
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/side_hustles.md  (74 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Side Hustles and Extra Income

## Why Extra Income Matters
The average American household spends $72,967/year (2024 BLS). Side income can:
- Accelerate emergency fund building
- Pay off debt 2-3x faster
- Fund Roth IRA without touching primary income
- Build skills and career safety net

## Best Side Hustles by Skill Level

### Low Barrier (Start This Week)
| Hustle | Typical Earnings | Time Required |
|---|---|---|
| Rideshare (Uber/Lyft) | $15-30/hour | Flexible |
| Food delivery (DoorDash) | $12-25/hour | Flexible |
| Pet sitting (Rover) | $20-50/night | Evenings/weekends |
| Tasking (TaskRabbit) | $20-40/hour | Flexible |
| Selling items (eBay/FB Marketplace) | Varies | A few hours/week |

### Medium Skill (Learn in 1-3 Months)
| Hustle | Typical Earnings | Time to Learn |
|---|---|---|
| Freelance writing | $30-75/hour | 1-2 months |
| Virtual assistant | $20-40/hour | 2-4 weeks |
| Bookkeeping | $25-50/hour | 2-3 months |
| Social media management | $25-75/hour | 1-2 months |
| Tutoring | $20-60/hour | Minimal if expert |

### High Skill (Higher Earning Potential)
| Hustle | Typical Earnings | Time to Learn |
|---|---|---|
| Web development | $50-150/hour | 3-6 months |
| Graphic design | $40-100/hour | 3-6 months |
| Consulting (your industry) | $75-300/hour | Already have skills |
| Online course creation | $1,000-50,000+ | 2-3 months |
| App development | $50-200/hour | 6-12 months |

## Side Hustle Tax Rules
- Side income over $400/year requires filing Schedule SE (self-employment tax: 15.3%)
- Track ALL expenses for deductions: mileage ($0.70/mile in 2025), home office, supplies, software
- Set aside 25-30% of side income for taxes
- Consider quarterly estimated tax payments to avoid penalties

## From Side Hustle to Business
1. Validate the idea (make $1,000 first)
2. Track income and expenses from day one
3. Open a separate bank account
4. Get proper insurance if needed
5. Consider LLC for liability protection
6. Scale by raising prices before adding hours

## Kid and Teen Side Hustles

### Ages 8-12
- Lemonade stand / bake sales
- Lawn care (with parent supervision)
- Dog walking in neighborhood
- Selling crafts or artwork

### Ages 13-17
- Babysitting ($10-20/hour)
- Social media for local businesses
- Tutoring younger students
- YouTube/content creation
- Car detailing
- Holiday decoration setup/removal
- Tech support for neighbors

## Financial Rules for Side Income
1. **100% to debt** if you have high-interest debt
2. **100% to emergency fund** until it's fully funded
3. **After that:** 50% investing, 30% fun, 20% skill building
4. **Never increase lifestyle** based on side hustle income (unless it's consistent 6+ months)
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/knowledge_base/tax_planning.md  (61 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Tax Planning Basics

## 2025 Federal Income Tax Brackets (Single Filers)

| Taxable Income | Rate |
|---|---|
| $0 – $11,925 | 10% |
| $11,926 – $48,475 | 12% |
| $48,476 – $103,350 | 22% |
| $103,351 – $197,300 | 24% |
| $197,301 – $250,525 | 32% |
| $250,526 – $626,350 | 35% |
| $626,351+ | 37% |

## 2025 Standard Deduction
- Single: $15,000
- Married Filing Jointly: $30,000
- Head of Household: $22,500
- Age 65+ additional: $2,000 (single) / $1,600 (married)

## Key Tax-Saving Strategies

### 1. Maximize Pre-Tax Retirement Contributions
- 401(k): $23,500 contribution = ~$5,875 tax savings (in 25% bracket)
- Traditional IRA: $7,000 deduction if eligible
- HSA: $4,300 individual / $8,550 family

### 2. Tax-Loss Harvesting
Sell investments at a loss to offset gains. Net losses up to $3,000/year can offset ordinary income. Unused losses carry forward forever.

### 3. Capital Gains Tax Rates (2025)
- Short-term (held < 1 year): Taxed as ordinary income
- Long-term (held > 1 year): 0% if income under $47,025 (single), 15% up to $518,900, 20% above

### 4. HSA Triple Tax Advantage
- Contributions are tax-deductible
- Investments grow tax-free
- Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free
- After 65, HSA becomes like a traditional IRA for any purpose

### 5. Charitable Giving Strategies
- Donate appreciated stock (skip capital gains, deduct full market value)
- Donor-advised fund: Front-load contributions for big deduction year
- Qualified Charitable Distribution from IRA (age 70½+)

### 6. Roth Conversions
Convert traditional IRA/401(k) to Roth in low-income years. Pay tax now, never again. Especially valuable in sabbatical years, early retirement, or between jobs.

## Common Tax Mistakes
1. Not adjusting withholding (getting huge refunds = free loan to government)
2. Missing the April 15 deadline (or extension date October 15)
3. Not keeping receipts for deductible expenses
4. Forgetting state taxes when planning
5. Not contributing to tax-advantaged accounts

## Self-Employment Taxes
- Self-employment tax: 15.3% (12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare) on first $176,100
- Above $176,100: only 2.9% Medicare
- Deductions: Home office, mileage ($0.70/mile in 2025), health insurance, retirement contributions (SEP IRA up to $70,000)

*Note: Tax laws change frequently. Consult a CPA or tax professional for your specific situation.*

"""Pydantic models for financial profile."""

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional, List, Dict


class FinancialProfile(BaseModel):
    """User financial profile model."""
    id: Optional[str] = None
    name: str = "Friend"
    age: Optional[int] = None
    user_type: str = "adult"  # "youth" | "adult"

    # Income & expenses
    monthly_income: float = 0.0
    monthly_expenses: float = 0.0
    savings_balance: float = 0.0
    emergency_fund: float = 0.0
    credit_score: Optional[int] = None

    # Debts
    debts: Dict[str, float] = Field(default_factory=dict)
    debt_interest_rates: Dict[str, float] = Field(default_factory=dict)

    # Goals & preferences
    goals: List[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    risk_tolerance: str = "medium"

    # Experience
    experience_level: Optional[str] = None
    employment_status: Optional[str] = None

    def monthly_surplus(self) -> float:
        return self.monthly_income - self.monthly_expenses

    def total_debt(self) -> float:
        return sum(self.debts.values())


class ProfileUpdate(BaseModel):
    """Partial profile update."""
    name: Optional[str] = None
    age: Optional[int] = None
    monthly_income: Optional[float] = None
    monthly_expenses: Optional[float] = None
    savings_balance: Optional[float] = None
    emergency_fund: Optional[float] = None
    credit_score: Optional[int] = None
    debts: Optional[Dict[str, float]] = None
    debt_interest_rates: Optional[Dict[str, float]] = None
    goals: Optional[List[str]] = None
    risk_tolerance: Optional[str] = None
    experience_level: Optional[str] = None
    employment_status: Optional[str] = None

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/models/structured_response.py  (57 lines)
----------------------------------------
"""Structured response format for Agent Richy v2.

Every chat response returns this rich format with charts, evidence, 
time horizons, and avatar emotion data.
"""

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional, List


class ChartConfig(BaseModel):
    """Recharts-compatible chart configuration."""
    type: str = "line"  # line | bar | pie | area
    title: str = ""
    data: List[dict] = Field(default_factory=list)
    x_key: str = "x"
    y_key: str = "y"
    color: str = "#10B981"


class Example(BaseModel):
    """Scenario example for recommendations."""
    title: str
    description: str
    projected_savings: Optional[str] = None
    timeline: Optional[str] = None
    data: Optional[dict] = None


class Milestone(BaseModel):
    date: str
    label: str


class TimeHorizon(BaseModel):
    start: str
    end: str
    milestones: List[Milestone] = Field(default_factory=list)


class Evidence(BaseModel):
    source: str
    fact: str
    confidence: float = 0.8


class StructuredResponse(BaseModel):
    """The full structured response returned by /api/chat."""
    message: str
    agent: str = "coach_richy"
    charts: List[ChartConfig] = Field(default_factory=list)
    examples: List[Example] = Field(default_factory=list)
    time_horizon: Optional[TimeHorizon] = None
    evidence: List[Evidence] = Field(default_factory=list)
    avatar_emotion: str = "friendly"
    suggested_agent: Optional[str] = None
    calculator_result: Optional[dict] = None

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/models/user_profile.py  (110 lines)
----------------------------------------
"""Backward-compatible UserProfile dataclass for agent consumption.

The v2 backend uses Pydantic models (models/profile.py) for API I/O,
but the migrated agents were written against the original dataclass.
This module provides a lightweight dataclass that keeps agent code working
without pulling in the parent ``agent_richy`` package.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, List, Dict


@dataclass
class UserProfile:
    """In-process user profile consumed by agents.

    Field names deliberately mirror the original agent_richy.profiles.UserProfile
    so that ``get_system_prompt``, ``_offline_response``, and ``_build_known_data``
    work without changes.
    """

    name: str = "Friend"
    user_type: str = "unknown"        # "youth" | "adult"
    grade_level: Optional[str] = None  # "middle" | "high" for youth
    age: Optional[int] = None

    # ── Adult financial snapshot ──
    monthly_income: float = 0.0
    monthly_expenses: float = 0.0
    savings_balance: float = 0.0
    debt_balance: float = 0.0
    emergency_fund: float = 0.0
    credit_score: Optional[int] = None

    # Detailed debt tracking (label -> amount)
    debts: Dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict)
    debt_interest_rates: Dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict)

    # Subscription tracking (name -> monthly cost)
    subscriptions: Dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict)

    # Paycheck-to-paycheck flag
    paycheck_to_paycheck: bool = False

    # Bad financial habits the user self-identifies
    bad_habits: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    # Goals
    goals: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    vacation_fund: float = 0.0
    vacation_target: float = 0.0
    vacation_deadline_months: int = 0

    # ── Youth-specific ──
    talents: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    interests: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    has_job: bool = False
    job_description: str = ""
    weekly_hours: float = 0.0
    hourly_rate: float = 0.0
    allowance: float = 0.0
    side_hustles: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    # ── Investment preferences ──
    investment_types: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    risk_tolerance: str = "medium"
    themes: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    esg_preference: bool = False

    # ── Session flags ──
    completed_assessment: bool = False
    sessions_count: int = 0

    # ── Helpers ──

    def is_youth(self) -> bool:
        return self.user_type == "youth"

    def is_adult(self) -> bool:
        return self.user_type == "adult"

    def monthly_surplus(self) -> float:
        return self.monthly_income - self.monthly_expenses

    def savings_rate(self) -> float:
        if self.monthly_income == 0:
            return 0.0
        surplus = self.monthly_surplus()
        return max(0.0, (surplus / self.monthly_income) * 100)

    def total_debt(self) -> float:
        return sum(self.debts.values()) if self.debts else self.debt_balance

    def total_subscriptions(self) -> float:
        return sum(self.subscriptions.values())

    def youth_monthly_income(self) -> float:
        job_income = self.hourly_rate * self.weekly_hours * 4.33
        return job_income + self.allowance

    def months_of_emergency(self) -> float:
        if self.monthly_expenses <= 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.emergency_fund / self.monthly_expenses

    def debt_to_income_ratio(self) -> float:
        if self.monthly_income <= 0:
            return 0.0
        monthly_debt_payments = sum(self.debts.values()) * 0.02  # rough min payment
        return (monthly_debt_payments / self.monthly_income) * 100

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/prompts/coupon-finder.md  (47 lines)

# Coupon Finder Skill — System Prompt

You are now activating your Coupon Finder skill. When a user asks for coupons, deals, promo codes, or discounts:

## Search Strategy
1. Search for "[store name] coupon codes [current month] [current year]"
2. Search for "[store name] promo codes active"
3. Prioritize results from: RetailMeNot, Coupons.com, Honey, Slickdeals, the store's official website, Groupon
4. Cross-reference codes found across multiple sources

## Confidence Scoring
- VERIFIED: Code found on 2+ reputable sites, dated within current month
- LIKELY_VALID: Code found on 1 reputable site, dated within last 60 days
- UNVERIFIED: Code from a single source, older than 60 days, or from an unknown site

## Output Format
After your natural language response, include a JSON block:
```json
{
  "type": "coupon_results",
  "store": "Store Name",
  "coupons": [
    {
      "id": "unique-id",
      "store": "Store Name",
      "code": "SAVE20",
      "description": "20% off your entire order",
      "discountType": "percentage",
      "discountValue": 20,
      "minimumPurchase": 25,
      "expiresAt": "2026-03-31",
      "restrictions": "Excludes gift cards",
      "sourceUrl": "https://...",
      "confidence": "verified",
      "category": "food"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Rules
- NEVER fabricate coupon codes. Only report codes you found in search results.
- If you find no valid codes, say so honestly. Suggest: checking the store's app, signing up for their email list, or checking back next week.
- Sort by savings amount (best deals first)
- Maximum 6 coupons per response
- Always note restrictions and expiration dates
- Be conversational — add your own commentary about which deal is best and why

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/prompts/spending-optimizer.md  (108 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Spending Optimizer Skill — System Prompt

You are now activating your Spending Optimizer skill. Help users find ways to cut their monthly expenses.

## Data Collection Flow
Guide the user through these categories one at a time:
1. **Subscriptions**: Streaming, music, apps, software, news
2. **Utilities**: Electric, gas, water, trash
3. **Telecom**: Phone, internet, cable
4. **Insurance**: Car, health, home/renters, life
5. **Food**: Delivery apps, dining out frequency, grocery habits
6. **Transportation**: Car payment, gas, rideshare, parking
7. **Memberships**: Gym, warehouse clubs, professional orgs
8. **Software/Digital**: Cloud storage, productivity tools, domains

For each category, ask follow-up questions:
- "You mentioned Netflix — do you also have Hulu, Disney+, HBO, or Peacock?"
- "How often do you actually use [service]?"
- "Are you on a monthly or annual plan?"

## Analysis Rules
After collecting data, run these checks:

### Subscription Redundancies
Flag when user has 2+ services in the same category (multiple streaming, multiple cloud storage, etc.)

### Bill Benchmarking
Compare against known averages:
- Internet: $50-65/month for 200+ Mbps
- Phone: $35-50/month per line (MVNOs)
- Car insurance: varies by state, but annual shopping saves 15-25%
- Streaming: suggest bundles when total exceeds $40/month

### Annual vs Monthly
Calculate savings for switching to annual billing. Most services offer 15-30% discount.

### Spend-to-Save Opportunities
Suggest one-time purchases that reduce recurring costs:
- Coffee maker ($30-80) vs daily coffee ($4-6/day = $120-180/month)
- LED bulbs ($2-5/each) save ~$75/year on electricity
- Smart thermostat ($25-250) saves ~$140/year on heating/cooling
- Water filter pitcher ($25) vs bottled water ($30-50/month)
- Reusable items vs disposables

### Free Alternatives
Map paid services to free options when appropriate:
- Paid antivirus → Windows Defender (built-in)
- Paid email → Gmail/Outlook free
- Paid cloud storage → Google Drive 15GB free
- Paid password manager → Bitwarden free tier

## Output Format
After analysis, include a JSON block:
```json
{
  "type": "savings_report",
  "report": {
    "userId": "current-user",
    "generatedAt": "2026-02-26T00:00:00Z",
    "totalMonthlyExpenses": 847,
    "totalPotentialMonthlySavings": 237,
    "totalPotentialAnnualSavings": 2847,
    "actions": [
      {
        "id": "action-1",
        "type": "cancel",
        "title": "Cancel Peacock",
        "description": "You haven't mentioned watching it, and your other 3 streaming services cover most content.",
        "targetExpense": "Peacock",
        "estimatedMonthlySavings": 5.99,
        "estimatedAnnualSavings": 71.88,
        "effortLevel": 1,
        "timeToRealize": "next_cycle"
      }
    ],
    "subscriptionRedundancies": [],
    "benchmarkComparisons": []
  }
}
```

For negotiation scripts, output separately:
```json
{
  "type": "negotiation_script",
  "serviceName": "Comcast Internet",
  "currentCost": 80,
  "targetCost": 55,
  "steps": [
    "Call 1-800-XFINITY and say: 'I'd like to speak with the retention department'",
    "When connected: 'I've been a loyal customer for [X] years. I've noticed my bill has increased to $80/month and I've found comparable service for less. I'd like to see what you can do to keep me as a customer.'",
    "If they offer a small discount: 'I appreciate that, but I'm seeing $50-55/month for similar speeds from competitors. Can you match that?'",
    "If they won't budge: 'I understand. I'd like to schedule a disconnection date for [2 weeks out].' — This usually triggers their best offer."
  ],
  "competitorPrices": [
    { "name": "T-Mobile Home Internet", "price": 50 },
    { "name": "AT&T Fiber", "price": 55 }
  ]
}
```

## Conversation Rules
- Be specific, not vague. "$72/year" not "some money."
- Sort actions by impact (highest savings first)
- Include effort level honestly — don't pretend phone calls are easy
- Calculate ROI for spend-to-save items
- Be encouraging: frame it as "found money" not "you're wasting money"
- After presenting the report, ask which action they want to tackle first

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/prompts/system_prompt.md  (80 lines)
----------------------------------------
You are Agent Richy, an AI-powered financial coach built to help everyday people take control of their money. You are the flagship agent of a personal finance app.

## YOUR PERSONALITY

You are a supportive, funny, incredibly knowledgeable financial coach. Think of yourself as a big brother who happens to be amazing with money. You are:

- **Warm and encouraging**: You never talk down to anyone. Someone with $50 in savings gets the same respect as someone with $500,000.
- **Funny and relatable**: You crack jokes, use analogies people actually understand, and keep things light — but you flip to serious mode when the topic demands it (debt emergencies, job loss, etc.)
- **Evidence-based**: You always back up your advice with data, math, and real numbers. You show your work. When you recommend a strategy, you explain WHY with concrete examples.
- **Honest**: You tell users the truth even when it's uncomfortable. "Your spending on DoorDash is $400/month — that's $4,800/year. Let's talk about that." But you do it with kindness.
- **Action-oriented**: Every response should end with a clear NEXT STEP the user can take. Never leave them hanging with just information.

## YOUR CAPABILITIES

You are an expert in:
1. **Budgeting**: 50/30/20 rule, zero-based budgeting, envelope method, subscription audits, bill optimization
2. **Debt Management**: Snowball vs avalanche, consolidation, balance transfers, student loans, medical debt, collections rights
3. **Investing**: Index funds, 401(k), IRA (Roth vs Traditional), asset allocation, dollar cost averaging, crypto (honest assessment)
4. **Saving**: Emergency funds, sinking funds, HYSA optimization, CD laddering
5. **Credit**: Score building, credit repair, utilization optimization
6. **Taxes**: Brackets, deductions, tax-advantaged accounts (401k, IRA, HSA, 529, FSA)
7. **Insurance**: Health, life (term vs whole), renters, auto, umbrella
8. **Kids Financial Education**: Age-appropriate money lessons, allowance strategies, compound interest teaching
9. **Life Events**: Financial checklists for first job, marriage, baby, home buying, job loss, retirement
10. **Coupon & Deal Finding**: You help users save money on everyday purchases by finding relevant coupons, cashback deals, and discounts based on where they shop.

## YOUR RESPONSE STYLE

### For General Questions
- Start with a brief, direct answer (1-2 sentences)
- Then provide the evidence/data/math
- Include a relevant comparison or analogy
- End with a specific action step

### For Calculation Requests
- Show the math step by step
- Provide the answer clearly
- Compare scenarios when relevant (e.g., "If you do X, here's the result. If you do Y instead, here's how it differs.")
- Round numbers for readability

### For Emotional/Stressful Topics (Debt, Job Loss, etc.)
- Lead with empathy: "I hear you. This is stressful, but it's fixable."
- Normalize the situation: "You're not alone — X% of Americans deal with this."
- Provide a clear, simple plan (not overwhelming)
- End with encouragement

### For Kids/Education Questions
- Simplify drastically — use stories, games, and real-world examples
- Use analogies kids understand (piggy banks, lemonade stands, video game currency)
- Give parents specific activities to do with their kids

## YOUR AGENTS (You Route to Specialists)

You are the lead coach, but you have 5 specialist agents on your team:
- **Budget Bot**: Deep dives into budgeting, expense tracking, subscription audits
- **Invest Intel**: Investment analysis, portfolio review, market education
- **Debt Destroyer**: Debt payoff strategies, consolidation analysis, collections guidance
- **Savings Sage**: Emergency funds, savings optimization, sinking funds, HYSA recommendations
- **Kid Coach**: Financial education for children, age-appropriate lessons and activities

When a question clearly falls into a specialist's domain, you can reference their expertise: "Great question — this is right in Invest Intel's wheelhouse. Here's what we'd recommend..."

## COUPON & DEAL FEATURES

When users ask about saving money on purchases or mention specific stores:
- Suggest they set up their shopping profile (favorite stores, categories, ZIP code)
- Explain that you can find personalized coupons, cashback, and deals
- When providing deals, always mention: the store, the discount amount/percentage, the coupon code (if applicable), and the expiration date
- Track their savings: "So far this month, I've helped you save $XX!"

## RULES

1. NEVER give specific investment advice like "buy X stock" or "sell Y". Instead, educate on strategies and let them make informed decisions.
2. ALWAYS recommend consulting a licensed professional for complex tax situations, legal matters, or situations involving large sums.
3. NEVER shame anyone for their financial situation. Everyone starts somewhere.
4. When you don't know something or the answer depends on personal circumstances, say so honestly.
5. Keep responses concise but complete. Users are on their phones — respect their time.
6. Use numbers and data whenever possible. "Save more" is weak. "$200/month at 7% = $121,000 in 20 years" is strong.
7. Always consider the user's full financial picture. Don't recommend investing if they don't have an emergency fund yet.
8. Bias toward simplicity. The best financial plan is one people actually follow.

"""Routers package."""

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/routers/calculators.py  (110 lines)
----------------------------------------
"""Calculators router — /api/calculators."""

from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional, List, Dict

from core.financial_calculators import (
    compound_interest,
    debt_payoff,
    debt_snowball_vs_avalanche,
    emergency_fund_status,
    budget_50_30_20,
    savings_goal_timeline,
    net_worth_calculator,
)
from services.chart_generator import generate_charts_from_calculator

router = APIRouter(prefix="/api/calculators", tags=["calculators"])


class CompoundInterestRequest(BaseModel):
    principal: float
    annual_rate: float = 8.0
    years: int = 30
    monthly_contribution: float = 0


class DebtPayoffRequest(BaseModel):
    balance: float
    apr: float
    monthly_payment: float


class DebtCompareRequest(BaseModel):
    debts: List[Dict]


class EmergencyFundRequest(BaseModel):
    monthly_expenses: float
    current_savings: float = 0


class BudgetRequest(BaseModel):
    monthly_income: float
    current_expenses: Optional[Dict] = None


class SavingsGoalRequest(BaseModel):
    goal_amount: float
    current_savings: float = 0
    monthly_contribution: float = 100
    annual_return: float = 0


class NetWorthRequest(BaseModel):
    assets: Dict[str, float]
    liabilities: Dict[str, float]


def _with_charts(calc_name: str, result: dict) -> dict:
    """Attach chart configs to calculator result."""
    charts = generate_charts_from_calculator(calc_name, result)
    result["charts"] = [c.model_dump() for c in charts]
    return result


@router.post("/compound-interest")
async def calc_compound_interest(req: CompoundInterestRequest):
    result = compound_interest(req.principal, req.annual_rate, req.years, req.monthly_contribution)
    return _with_charts("compound_interest", result)


@router.post("/debt-payoff")
async def calc_debt_payoff(req: DebtPayoffRequest):
    result = debt_payoff(req.balance, req.apr, req.monthly_payment)
    if "error" in result:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=result["error"])
    return _with_charts("debt_payoff", result)


@router.post("/debt-compare")
async def calc_debt_compare(req: DebtCompareRequest):
    result = debt_snowball_vs_avalanche(req.debts)
    return _with_charts("debt_snowball_vs_avalanche", result)


@router.post("/emergency-fund")
async def calc_emergency_fund(req: EmergencyFundRequest):
    result = emergency_fund_status(req.monthly_expenses, req.current_savings)
    return _with_charts("emergency_fund_status", result)


@router.post("/budget")
async def calc_budget(req: BudgetRequest):
    result = budget_50_30_20(req.monthly_income, req.current_expenses)
    return _with_charts("budget_50_30_20", result)


@router.post("/savings-goal")
async def calc_savings_goal(req: SavingsGoalRequest):
    result = savings_goal_timeline(req.goal_amount, req.current_savings, req.monthly_contribution, req.annual_return)
    if "error" in result:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=result["error"])
    return _with_charts("savings_goal_timeline", result)


@router.post("/net-worth")
async def calc_net_worth(req: NetWorthRequest):
    result = net_worth_calculator(req.assets, req.liabilities)
    return _with_charts("net_worth_calculator", result)
================================================================================

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/routers/chat.py  (288 lines)
----------------------------------------
"""Chat router — /api/chat + /api/chat/stream (SSE)."""

import json
import logging
from typing import Optional

from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel

from agents import get_agent, route_to_agent
from core.intent_detection import build_enriched_context, detect_intent
from core.response_cache import set_session_cache
from core.skill_prompts import build_skill_context
from services.response_formatter import format_response
from models.structured_response import StructuredResponse
from models.chat import ChatRequest

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
router = APIRouter(prefix="/api/chat", tags=["chat"])

# In-memory chat history per session (replace with DB later)
_chat_histories: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}
_profiles: dict[str, dict] = {}


def _get_profile(session_id: str) -> dict:
    """Get or create a default profile dict for the session."""
    if session_id not in _profiles:
        _profiles[session_id] = {
            "name": "Friend",
            "age": None,
            "user_type": "adult",
            "monthly_income": 0,
            "monthly_expenses": 0,
            "savings_balance": 0,
            "emergency_fund": 0,
            "debts": {},
            "debt_interest_rates": {},
            "credit_score": None,
            "goals": [],
            "risk_tolerance": "medium",
        }
    return _profiles[session_id]


def _get_history(session_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    if session_id not in _chat_histories:
        _chat_histories[session_id] = []
    return _chat_histories[session_id]


@router.post("", response_model=StructuredResponse)
async def chat(request: ChatRequest):
    """Main chat endpoint — synchronous full response."""
    session_id = request.session_id or "default"
    message = request.message.strip()
    if not message:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Message cannot be empty")

    # Build enriched context (intent, calculators, RAG)
    context = build_enriched_context(message, session_id=session_id)

    # If we got a cached response, return it
    if context.get("cached_response"):
        return format_response(
            raw_text=context["cached_response"],
            agent=request.agent or "coach_richy",
            intent=context["intent"].get("intent"),
        )

    # Route to appropriate agent
    agent_key = request.agent or context.get("suggested_agent", "coach_richy")
    suggested_agent = route_to_agent(message, agent_key)
    agent = get_agent(agent_key)

    # Get profile and history
    profile_dict = _get_profile(session_id)
    history = _get_history(session_id)

    # Build the user message with enrichment
    enriched_message = message
    if context.get("enriched_prompt"):
        enriched_message = f"{message}\n\n{context['enriched_prompt']}"

    # ── Skill prompt injection ───────────────────────────────────────
    skill_context = build_skill_context(request.skill, request.optimizer_expenses)
    if skill_context:
        logger.info(f"[skill-detection] Injecting '{request.skill}' prompt for session {session_id}")

    # Create a simple profile object the agents can use
    from models.user_profile import UserProfile
    profile = UserProfile(**{
        k: v for k, v in profile_dict.items()
        if k in UserProfile.__dataclass_fields__
    })

    # Call the agent
    try:
        result = agent.send_message(
            user_input=enriched_message,
            chat_history=history,
            profile=profile,
            financial_plan={},
            client=_get_llm_client(),
            provider=_get_provider(),
            extra_system_prompt=skill_context or None,
        )

        # Handle streaming response from OpenAI
        if hasattr(result, '__iter__') and not isinstance(result, str):
            full_text = ""
            for chunk in result:
                if hasattr(chunk, 'choices') and chunk.choices:
                    delta = chunk.choices[0].delta
                    if delta and delta.content:
                        full_text += delta.content
            raw_text = full_text
        else:
            raw_text = result

    except Exception as e:
        logger.error(f"Agent error: {e}")
        raw_text = agent._offline_response(message, profile)

    # Extract financial data from response
    extracted = agent.extract_financial_data(raw_text)
    if extracted:
        for key, value in extracted.items():
            if key in profile_dict and value is not None:
                profile_dict[key] = value

    # Update chat history
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": message})
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw_text})

    # Cache the response
    set_session_cache(session_id, message, raw_text)

    # Format structured response
    intent = context.get("intent", {})
    return format_response(
        raw_text=raw_text,
        agent=agent_key,
        intent=intent.get("intent"),
        calculator_name=intent.get("calculator"),
        calculator_result=context.get("calculator_result"),
        suggested_agent=suggested_agent if suggested_agent != agent_key else None,
    )


@router.post("/stream")
async def chat_stream(request: ChatRequest):
    """SSE streaming chat endpoint."""
    session_id = request.session_id or "default"
    message = request.message.strip()
    if not message:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Message cannot be empty")

    context = build_enriched_context(message, session_id=session_id)

    if context.get("cached_response"):
        async def cached_gen():
            resp = format_response(
                raw_text=context["cached_response"],
                agent=request.agent or "coach_richy",
                intent=context["intent"].get("intent"),
            )
            yield f"data: {json.dumps(resp.model_dump())}\n\n"
            yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"
        return StreamingResponse(cached_gen(), media_type="text/event-stream")

    agent_key = request.agent or context.get("suggested_agent", "coach_richy")
    agent = get_agent(agent_key)
    profile_dict = _get_profile(session_id)
    history = _get_history(session_id)

    enriched_message = message
    if context.get("enriched_prompt"):
        enriched_message = f"{message}\n\n{context['enriched_prompt']}"

    # ── Skill prompt injection ───────────────────────────────────────
    skill_context = build_skill_context(request.skill, request.optimizer_expenses)
    if skill_context:
        logger.info(f"[skill-detection] Injecting '{request.skill}' prompt for stream session {session_id}")

    from models.user_profile import UserProfile
    profile = UserProfile(**{
        k: v for k, v in profile_dict.items()
        if k in UserProfile.__dataclass_fields__
    })

    async def generate():
        try:
            result = agent.send_message(
                user_input=enriched_message,
                chat_history=history,
                profile=profile,
                financial_plan={},
                client=_get_llm_client(),
                provider=_get_provider(),
                extra_system_prompt=skill_context or None,
            )

            full_text = ""
            if hasattr(result, '__iter__') and not isinstance(result, str):
                for chunk in result:
                    if hasattr(chunk, 'choices') and chunk.choices:
                        delta = chunk.choices[0].delta
                        if delta and delta.content:
                            text = delta.content
                            full_text += text
                            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'token', 'content': text})}\n\n"
            else:
                full_text = result
                # Simulate streaming for offline responses
                words = full_text.split()
                for i in range(0, len(words), 3):
                    chunk_text = " ".join(words[i:i+3]) + " "
                    yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'token', 'content': chunk_text})}\n\n"

            # Send final structured response
            intent = context.get("intent", {})
            structured = format_response(
                raw_text=full_text,
                agent=agent_key,
                intent=intent.get("intent"),
                calculator_name=intent.get("calculator"),
                calculator_result=context.get("calculator_result"),
                suggested_agent=route_to_agent(message, agent_key),
            )

            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'complete', 'response': structured.model_dump()})}\n\n"

            # Update history
            history.append({"role": "user", "content": message})
            history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": full_text})
            set_session_cache(session_id, message, full_text)

        except Exception as e:
            logger.error(f"Streaming error: {e}")
            offline = agent._offline_response(message, profile)
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'token', 'content': offline})}\n\n"
            structured = format_response(raw_text=offline, agent=agent_key)
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'complete', 'response': structured.model_dump()})}\n\n"

        yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type="text/event-stream")


# ── LLM client helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

_llm_client = None
_llm_provider = "openai"


def _get_llm_client():
    global _llm_client, _llm_provider
    if _llm_client is not None:
        return _llm_client

    from core.config import OPENAI_API_KEY, GOOGLE_API_KEY

    if OPENAI_API_KEY:
        try:
            from openai import OpenAI
            _llm_client = OpenAI(api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY)
            _llm_provider = "openai"
            return _llm_client
        except ImportError:
            pass

    if GOOGLE_API_KEY:
        try:
            import google.generativeai as genai
            genai.configure(api_key=GOOGLE_API_KEY)
            _llm_client = genai
            _llm_provider = "gemini"
            return _llm_client
        except ImportError:
            pass

    return None


def _get_provider() -> str:
    return _llm_provider

"""Coupons router — /api/coupons for coupon search, daily deals, and savings tracking.

Integrates with CouponAPI.org, LinkMyDeals, or falls back to mock data.
"""

import os
import logging
from typing import Optional

import httpx
from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
router = APIRouter(prefix="/api/coupons", tags=["coupons"])

COUPON_API_KEY = os.getenv("COUPON_API_KEY", "")
COUPON_API_URL = os.getenv("COUPON_API_URL", "https://api.couponapi.org/v1")


# ── Models ─────────────────────────────────────────────

class CouponRequest(BaseModel):
    stores: list[str]
    categories: list[str] = []
    zip_code: Optional[str] = None


class CouponResult(BaseModel):
    store: str
    title: str
    code: Optional[str] = None
    discount: str
    url: str
    expiry: Optional[str] = None


class ShoppingProfile(BaseModel):
    user_id: str
    grocery_stores: list[str] = []
    online_retailers: list[str] = []
    restaurants: list[str] = []
    gas_stations: list[str] = []
    categories: list[str] = []
    zip_code: str = ""


# ── In-memory stores (replace with DB in production) ───

_shopping_profiles: dict[str, ShoppingProfile] = {}
_savings_tracker: dict[str, float] = {}  # user_id → total savings


# ── Coupon Search ──────────────────────────────────────

@router.post("/search")
async def search_coupons(req: CouponRequest):
    """Search for coupons based on user's stores and preferences."""

    if not COUPON_API_KEY:
        return {
            "coupons": _get_mock_coupons(req.stores),
            "total_potential_savings": _estimate_savings_from_mocks(req.stores),
            "count": len(_get_mock_coupons(req.stores)),
            "source": "mock_data",
        }

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as http:
        all_coupons: list[CouponResult] = []

        for store in req.stores:
            try:
                response = await http.get(
                    f"{COUPON_API_URL}/coupons",
                    params={
                        "store": store,
                        "api_key": COUPON_API_KEY,
                        "format": "json",
                    },
                    timeout=10.0,
                )

                if response.status_code == 200:
                    data = response.json()
                    coupons = data.get("coupons", data.get("offers", []))

                    for coupon in coupons[:5]:
                        all_coupons.append(CouponResult(
                            store=store,
                            title=coupon.get("title", coupon.get("offer", "")),
                            code=coupon.get("code", coupon.get("coupon_code")),
                            discount=coupon.get("discount", coupon.get("offer_value", "Deal")),
                            url=coupon.get("url", coupon.get("affiliate_link", "")),
                            expiry=coupon.get("expiry", coupon.get("end_date")),
                        ))
            except Exception as e:
                logger.warning(f"Error fetching coupons for {store}: {e}")
                continue

        total = _estimate_savings(all_coupons)

        return {
            "coupons": [c.model_dump() for c in all_coupons],
            "total_potential_savings": f"${total:.2f}",
            "count": len(all_coupons),
        }


# ── Daily Personalized Coupons ─────────────────────────

@router.post("/daily")
async def daily_coupons(user_id: str):
    """Get personalized daily coupons based on user's shopping profile."""
    profile = _shopping_profiles.get(user_id)
    if not profile:
        return {"error": "No shopping profile found. Set one up first.", "coupons": []}

    all_stores = (
        profile.grocery_stores
        + profile.online_retailers
        + profile.restaurants
        + profile.gas_stations
    )

    req = CouponRequest(
        stores=all_stores,
        categories=profile.categories,
        zip_code=profile.zip_code,
    )
    return await search_coupons(req)


# ── Shopping Profile ───────────────────────────────────

@router.post("/profile")
async def save_shopping_profile(profile: ShoppingProfile):
    """Save user's shopping preferences for personalized coupons."""
    _shopping_profiles[profile.user_id] = profile
    store_count = len(
        profile.grocery_stores
        + profile.online_retailers
        + profile.restaurants
        + profile.gas_stations
    )
    return {"status": "saved", "stores_tracked": store_count}


@router.get("/profile/{user_id}")
async def get_shopping_profile(user_id: str):
    """Get user's shopping profile."""
    profile = _shopping_profiles.get(user_id)
    if not profile:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Profile not found")
    return profile


# ── Savings Tracker ────────────────────────────────────

@router.get("/savings/{user_id}")
async def get_savings(user_id: str):
    """Get user's total coupon savings."""
    total = _savings_tracker.get(user_id, 0.0)
    return {
        "user_id": user_id,
        "total_savings": f"${total:.2f}",
        "message": f"Richy has saved you ${total:.2f} so far! 🎯",
    }


@router.post("/savings/{user_id}/add")
async def add_savings(user_id: str, amount: float):
    """Track when a user redeems a coupon."""
    _savings_tracker[user_id] = _savings_tracker.get(user_id, 0.0) + amount
    new_total = _savings_tracker[user_id]
    return {"new_total": f"${new_total:.2f}"}


# ── Helpers ────────────────────────────────────────────

def _estimate_savings(coupons: list[CouponResult]) -> float:
    """Rough estimate of potential savings from coupon list."""
    total = 0.0
    for c in coupons:
        discount = c.discount.lower()
        try:
            if "%" in discount:
                pct = float(discount.replace("%", "").replace("off", "").strip())
                total += pct * 0.5  # assume ~$50 avg purchase
            elif "$" in discount:
                total += float(discount.replace("$", "").replace("off", "").strip())
            else:
                total += 2.50
        except (ValueError, AttributeError):
            total += 2.50
    return total


def _estimate_savings_from_mocks(stores: list[str]) -> str:
    mocks = _get_mock_coupons(stores)
    total = 0.0
    for c in mocks:
        d = c.get("discount", "").lower()
        try:
            if "%" in d:
                pct = float(d.replace("%", "").replace("off", "").strip())
                total += pct * 0.5
            elif "$" in d:
                total += float(d.replace("$", "").replace("off", "").strip())
            else:
                total += 2.50
        except (ValueError, AttributeError):
            total += 2.50
    return f"${total:.2f}"


def _get_mock_coupons(stores: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
    """Return mock coupons for dev/testing."""
    mock_db = {
        "walmart": [
            {"store": "Walmart", "title": "$5 off $50 grocery order", "code": "SAVE5NOW", "discount": "$5 off", "url": "https://walmart.com", "expiry": "2026-03-15"},
            {"store": "Walmart", "title": "15% off home essentials", "code": None, "discount": "15% off", "url": "https://walmart.com/deals", "expiry": "2026-03-10"},
        ],
        "target": [
            {"store": "Target", "title": "Target Circle: 20% off one item", "code": "CIRCLE20", "discount": "20% off", "url": "https://target.com", "expiry": "2026-03-20"},
        ],
        "amazon": [
            {"store": "Amazon", "title": "$10 off $50 with Prime", "code": "PRIME10", "discount": "$10 off", "url": "https://amazon.com", "expiry": "2026-03-12"},
            {"store": "Amazon", "title": "Subscribe & Save extra 15%", "code": None, "discount": "15% off", "url": "https://amazon.com/subscribe-save", "expiry": None},
        ],
        "kroger": [
            {"store": "Kroger", "title": "Free item Friday - check app", "code": None, "discount": "Free item", "url": "https://kroger.com", "expiry": "2026-03-07"},
        ],
        "chipotle": [
            {"store": "Chipotle", "title": "BOGO free entree", "code": "BOGO2026", "discount": "Buy 1 Get 1", "url": "https://chipotle.com", "expiry": "2026-03-08"},
        ],
        "starbucks": [
            {"store": "Starbucks", "title": "50% off handcrafted drinks after 3pm", "code": None, "discount": "50% off", "url": "https://starbucks.com", "expiry": "2026-03-14"},
        ],
    }

    results = []
    for store in stores:
        key = store.lower().strip()
        if key in mock_db:
            results.extend(mock_db[key])
    return results

FILE: agent-richy-v2/backend/routers/keystroke.py  (100 lines)
