FILE: data/knowledge_base/01_budgeting.md  (82 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Budgeting & Cash Flow Mastery

## The 50/30/20 Rule

The simplest budgeting framework that actually works. Split your after-tax income:
- **50% Needs**: Rent/mortgage, utilities, groceries, insurance, minimum debt payments, transportation
- **30% Wants**: Dining out, entertainment, subscriptions, shopping, hobbies
- **20% Savings & Debt**: Emergency fund, investments, extra debt payments

### By Income Level
| Gross Income | After Tax (est.) | Needs (50%) | Wants (30%) | Save/Debt (20%) |
|-------------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| $30,000 | $2,100/mo | $1,050 | $630 | $420 |
| $50,000 | $3,350/mo | $1,675 | $1,005 | $670 |
| $75,000 | $4,800/mo | $2,400 | $1,440 | $960 |
| $100,000 | $6,250/mo | $3,125 | $1,875 | $1,250 |

**Richy's Take**: "If your needs are eating more than 50%, don't panic. That's where we start cutting. Let me help you audit your fixed expenses first."

## Zero-Based Budgeting

Every dollar gets a job. Income minus ALL assigned categories = $0.

Steps:
1. Write your total monthly income at the top
2. List every expense category (rent, food, gas, etc.)
3. Assign a dollar amount to each until you hit $0
4. Track actual spending against each category
5. Adjust monthly based on what actually happened

Best for: People who want maximum control and are willing to track closely.

## Envelope Budgeting (Digital Version)

The old-school method, modernized. Create virtual "envelopes" for variable spending:
- Groceries: $400/mo
- Dining out: $150/mo
- Gas: $200/mo
- Fun money: $100/mo

When an envelope is empty, you stop spending in that category until next month. Apps like YNAB, Goodbudget, and EveryDollar automate this.

## Subscription Audit Checklist

Average American spends $219/month on subscriptions. Run this audit:
- [ ] List every recurring charge (check bank statements for last 3 months)
- [ ] Mark each: ESSENTIAL / NICE-TO-HAVE / FORGOT I HAD THIS
- [ ] Cancel everything in the "forgot" category immediately
- [ ] Negotiate or downgrade "nice-to-have" items
- [ ] Check for free alternatives (library apps for audiobooks, free Spotify tier, etc.)

Common hidden subscriptions: cloud storage, old streaming trials, gym memberships, app subscriptions, insurance add-ons, premium app tiers.

## Paycheck-to-Paycheck Escape Plan

### 30-Day Sprint
- Track every dollar spent (use an app or notebook)
- Identify your top 3 "money leaks"
- Cut one unnecessary expense completely
- Set up auto-transfer of $25/paycheck to savings (even if it feels small)

### 60-Day Milestone
- Build a $500 starter emergency fund
- Negotiate one bill (call and ask for a lower rate)
- Start the debt snowball on your smallest debt
- Increase auto-savings to $50/paycheck

### 90-Day Transformation
- Emergency fund hits $1,000
- One debt fully paid off (or close)
- Subscription costs reduced by 30%+
- You now have a working budget you actually follow

## Monthly Bill Calendar Strategy

Align your bills with your pay schedule to avoid cash crunches:

**If paid bi-weekly**: Split bills into "1st paycheck" and "2nd paycheck" groups. Put rent/mortgage on the larger paycheck. Spread utilities across both.

**If paid monthly**: Pay all bills within the first 5 days. What's left is your true spending money. No surprises.

**Richy's Pro Tip**: "Set up autopay for everything that has a fixed amount. For variable bills (electricity, water), set up autopay for the estimated average. You'll never miss a payment again, and your credit score will thank you."
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/02_debt_management.md  (103 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Debt Management & Elimination

## Snowball vs Avalanche Method

### Snowball Method (Behavioral Winner)
Pay minimum on all debts. Throw every extra dollar at the SMALLEST balance first.
- Pro: Quick wins keep you motivated
- Pro: Harvard Business Review found 15% higher completion rate
- Con: You may pay more total interest
- Best for: People who need momentum and motivation

### Avalanche Method (Math Winner)
Pay minimum on all debts. Throw every extra dollar at the HIGHEST INTEREST RATE first.
- Pro: Saves the most money in total interest
- Con: Can feel slow if your highest-rate debt is also your largest
- Best for: People who are disciplined and motivated by math

### Example Comparison
Debts: Credit card ($3,000 at 22%), Car loan ($8,000 at 6%), Student loan ($15,000 at 5%)
Extra payment available: $200/month

| Method | Order | Total Interest Paid | Time to Debt-Free |
|--------|-------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Snowball | CC → Car → Student | $4,280 | 34 months |
| Avalanche | CC → Car → Student | $3,950 | 33 months |

In this example, avalanche saves $330. But if snowball keeps you going and avalanche makes you quit, snowball wins every time.

**Richy's Take**: "Honestly? Pick the one you'll actually stick with. A perfect plan you abandon is worse than a good plan you finish."

## Debt Consolidation

Combining multiple debts into one payment. Works when:
- ✅ New interest rate is LOWER than your current weighted average
- ✅ You won't run up the old cards again
- ✅ Monthly payment is manageable
- ✅ Fees don't eat the savings

Does NOT work when:
- ❌ You consolidate but keep spending on credit
- ❌ The new rate is only temporarily low (watch for teaser rates)
- ❌ You extend the term so much that total interest increases

Options: Balance transfer cards (0% intro APR for 12-21 months), personal loans, home equity loans (risky — your house is collateral).

## Balance Transfer Strategy

1. Check your credit score (need 670+ for best offers)
2. Find a 0% intro APR card (typically 12-21 months)
3. Calculate the transfer fee (usually 3-5% of balance)
4. Transfer your highest-rate balances
5. Divide total balance by number of 0% months = your monthly payment
6. Set up autopay for that amount
7. DO NOT use the new card for purchases
8. Pay it off BEFORE the intro period ends (rates jump to 18-25%)

## Student Loan Repayment Options

| Plan | Payment Based On | Monthly Payment | Forgiveness |
|------|-----------------|----------------|-------------|
| Standard | Fixed | Highest | None (10 yr) |
| Graduated | Starts low, increases | Medium | None (10 yr) |
| IBR | 10-15% discretionary income | Lower | 20-25 years |
| PAYE | 10% discretionary income | Lower | 20 years |
| SAVE/REPAYE | 5-10% discretionary income | Lowest | 20-25 years |
| PSLF | Any IDR plan + public service | Varies | 10 years |

**Key insight**: If you work for government or nonprofits, PSLF (Public Service Loan Forgiveness) can erase your remaining balance after 120 qualifying payments. This is potentially worth tens of thousands of dollars.

## Medical Debt Negotiation Playbook

1. Request an itemized bill (hospitals frequently have errors)
2. Check for financial assistance programs (most hospitals have them)
3. Negotiate the total amount (offer 25-50% of the bill as a lump sum)
4. If they won't reduce, negotiate a 0% payment plan
5. NEVER put medical debt on a credit card (you lose all negotiation leverage)
6. Know your rights: Medical debt under $500 no longer appears on credit reports. New rules protect you.

## Collections: Know Your Rights (FDCPA)

The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act protects you:
- Collectors CANNOT call before 8am or after 9pm
- Collectors CANNOT threaten you with jail
- Collectors CANNOT discuss your debt with others (except spouse)
- You have the right to request written verification of the debt
- You can send a cease-and-desist letter to stop calls
- Debt has a statute of limitations (varies by state, typically 3-6 years)
- Making a payment can RESTART the statute of limitations clock

**Richy's Warning**: "Never acknowledge a debt is yours over the phone until you've verified it in writing. Some collectors buy old debts for pennies and try to collect on stuff that's already expired or isn't even yours."

## Debt-to-Income Ratio Targets

DTI = (Total Monthly Debt Payments / Gross Monthly Income) × 100

| DTI Range | Rating | What It Means |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Under 20% | Excellent | You're in great shape |
| 20-35% | Good | Manageable, but watch it |
| 36-49% | Concerning | Start aggressive payoff plan |
| 50%+ | Critical | Seek professional help, consider consolidation |

Mortgage lenders typically want DTI under 43% to approve you.
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/03_investing.md  (114 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Investing Fundamentals & Strategy

## The Investing Roadmap by Age

### Ages 18-25: Foundation Building
- Priority: Emergency fund first ($1,000 minimum, then 3 months expenses)
- Start with: Employer 401(k) up to match (it's FREE money)
- Then: Roth IRA (contribute post-tax, withdraw tax-free in retirement)
- Allocation: 90% stocks / 10% bonds (you have decades to recover from dips)
- Best vehicles: Target-date funds or total market index funds
- Amount: Even $50/month matters. $50/mo at 10% avg return = $379,000 by age 65

### Ages 25-35: Acceleration
- Max out Roth IRA ($7,000/year as of 2025)
- Increase 401(k) contributions toward 15% of income
- Consider taxable brokerage for additional investing
- Start thinking about home purchase (if it makes sense financially)
- Allocation: 80% stocks / 20% bonds

### Ages 35-50: Peak Earning Years
- Max out all tax-advantaged accounts
- Catch-up on retirement if behind (use the "multiply by 25" rule)
- Diversify: real estate, international stocks, bonds
- Allocation: 70% stocks / 30% bonds (adjust based on risk tolerance)

### Ages 50+: Preservation & Income
- Take advantage of catch-up contributions ($7,500 extra for 401k, $1,000 for IRA)
- Shift toward income-producing investments
- Allocation: 50-60% stocks / 40-50% bonds
- Start planning withdrawal strategy

## Index Funds vs Individual Stocks

| Factor | Index Funds | Individual Stocks |
|--------|------------|-------------------|
| Diversification | Instant (500+ companies in S&P 500) | You pick, concentrated risk |
| Fees | Very low (0.03% for VTI/VOO) | Trading fees + research time |
| Returns | Market average (~10%/yr historically) | Could beat OR lose to market |
| Time required | Set and forget | Hours of research weekly |
| Success rate | 90% of pros can't beat index funds over 15 years | Very few individuals beat long-term |

**Richy's Take**: "Index funds are boring. And boring is EXACTLY what makes you rich. The S&P 500 has returned ~10% annually over the last 100 years. You don't need to pick winners."

## Roth IRA vs Traditional IRA

| Factor | Roth IRA | Traditional IRA |
|--------|----------|-----------------|
| Tax on contributions | You pay taxes NOW | Tax deduction NOW |
| Tax on withdrawals | TAX-FREE in retirement | Taxed as income |
| Income limits (2025) | $161K single / $240K married | No limit (but deduction phases out) |
| Contribution limit | $7,000/yr ($8,000 if 50+) | Same |
| Required distributions | NONE (ever) | Must start at age 73 |
| Best if | You expect higher taxes later | You expect lower taxes later |
| Best for ages | Under 35 (lower tax bracket now) | Over 50 (higher bracket now) |

**The hack**: If you earn too much for a Roth IRA, use a "Backdoor Roth" — contribute to Traditional IRA, then immediately convert to Roth.

## 401(k) Match: The Free Money Guide

If your employer matches 401(k) contributions, ALWAYS contribute at least enough to get the full match. This is a 50-100% instant return on your money.

Example: Employer matches 50% up to 6% of your salary.
- Your salary: $60,000
- You contribute 6%: $3,600/year
- Employer adds 50% match: $1,800/year
- Total going into your 401(k): $5,400/year
- That $1,800 match is FREE. Not contributing enough to get it is literally leaving money on the table.

## Dollar Cost Averaging (DCA)

Invest a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of market price.

Why it works:
- When prices are HIGH, your fixed amount buys fewer shares
- When prices are LOW, your fixed amount buys MORE shares
- Over time, this averages out your cost per share
- Removes emotion from investing (no trying to "time the market")

Historical proof: An investor who put $500/month into the S&P 500 from 2000-2020 (through TWO major crashes) would have invested $120,000 and ended with approximately $345,000.

## Asset Allocation by Risk Tolerance

| Profile | Stocks | Bonds | Cash | Best For |
|---------|--------|-------|------|----------|
| Aggressive | 90% | 10% | 0% | 20+ year horizon, can stomach 40% drops |
| Moderate Growth | 70% | 25% | 5% | 10-20 year horizon |
| Balanced | 50% | 40% | 10% | 5-10 year horizon |
| Conservative | 30% | 50% | 20% | Under 5 years or near retirement |

## Crypto: What You Actually Need to Know

1. Crypto is speculative, NOT investing in the traditional sense
2. Never invest more than you can afford to lose completely
3. Bitcoin and Ethereum are the most established — everything else is higher risk
4. Dollar cost average if you want exposure (don't lump sum)
5. Keep crypto to 5% or less of your total portfolio
6. Use a hardware wallet for significant holdings
7. Understand: past returns don't guarantee future returns
8. Tax implications: every sale is a taxable event

**Richy's Honest Take**: "I won't tell you to avoid crypto. But I WILL tell you to max out your 401(k) match, build your emergency fund, and pay off high-interest debt FIRST. Then, if you want to put 5% into crypto with money you'd be okay losing? Go for it."

## Tax-Loss Harvesting

Selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains taxes.

How it works:
1. Sell an investment that's down (realizing the loss)
2. Use that loss to offset gains from other investments
3. Can offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income per year
4. Excess losses carry forward to future years
5. Buy a SIMILAR (but not identical) investment to maintain your position

**Important**: The wash-sale rule prevents you from buying the "substantially identical" security within 30 days. Buy something similar but not the same (e.g., sell VTI, buy ITOT).
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/04_savings_credit_taxes_insurance.md  (152 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Savings & Emergency Funds

## Emergency Fund Calculator
Target = Monthly Essential Expenses × Months of Coverage

| Job Stability | Recommended Coverage |
|--------------|---------------------|
| Government/tenured | 3 months |
| Stable full-time job | 3-6 months |
| Freelance/gig/commission | 6-9 months |
| Single income household | 6-9 months |
| Self-employed | 9-12 months |

Essential expenses include: rent/mortgage, utilities, food, insurance, transportation, minimum debt payments. Do NOT include wants like dining out or entertainment.

## Where to Keep Your Emergency Fund
- High-Yield Savings Account (HYSA): Currently 4-5% APY at online banks
- Money Market Account: Similar rates, sometimes with check-writing
- NOT in stocks, crypto, or CDs (need instant access)
- NOT under your mattress (inflation eats it)

Top HYSA options to research: Marcus (Goldman Sachs), Ally Bank, Capital One 360, Discover, Wealthfront Cash

## Sinking Funds
Save for predictable large expenses by setting aside money monthly:
- Car repairs: $100/mo → $1,200/year ready when something breaks
- Holiday gifts: $50/mo → $600 by December
- Vacation: $150/mo → $1,800 for a nice trip
- Car replacement: $200/mo → building toward next down payment

## CD Laddering Strategy
Split savings across CDs with staggered maturity dates:
- $2,000 in a 3-month CD
- $2,000 in a 6-month CD
- $2,000 in a 12-month CD
- $2,000 in a 18-month CD

As each matures, reinvest into a new 18-month CD. You get higher rates while maintaining regular access to portions of your money.

---

# Credit & Credit Scores

## How Credit Scores Work

### FICO Score Breakdown
| Factor | Weight | How to Optimize |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| Payment History | 35% | Never miss a payment. Set up autopay. |
| Credit Utilization | 30% | Keep balances below 30% of limit (10% is ideal) |
| Length of History | 15% | Don't close old cards. Age matters. |
| Credit Mix | 10% | Having different types (cards, loans, mortgage) helps |
| New Inquiries | 10% | Don't apply for multiple cards at once |

### Score Ranges
| Range | Rating | What You Get |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| 800-850 | Exceptional | Best rates on everything |
| 740-799 | Very Good | Excellent rates |
| 670-739 | Good | Decent rates, most approvals |
| 580-669 | Fair | Higher rates, some denials |
| Under 580 | Poor | Difficulty getting approved |

## Building Credit from Zero
1. Get a secured credit card ($200-500 deposit)
2. Use it for ONE small recurring charge (Netflix, gas)
3. Set up autopay for full balance
4. After 6-12 months, request upgrade to unsecured card
5. Alternative: Become an authorized user on a family member's card (their good history helps you)
6. Credit builder loans (Self, MoneyLion) report payments to bureaus

## Credit Repair: Legit Steps
1. Get free credit reports (AnnualCreditReport.com)
2. Dispute any errors in writing (30-day response required)
3. Pay down high-utilization cards first
4. Negotiate "pay for delete" on collections
5. DO NOT pay credit repair companies (you can do everything they do for free)
6. Time heals: most negatives fall off after 7 years, bankruptcies after 10

---

# Tax Essentials

## Tax Brackets Explained (It's Not What You Think)
Common misconception: "If I earn more, ALL my income gets taxed at the higher rate."
Reality: Only the income WITHIN each bracket is taxed at that rate.

Example (2025 Single Filer):
- First $11,600 → taxed at 10% = $1,160
- $11,601 - $47,150 → taxed at 12% = $4,266
- $47,151 - $100,525 → taxed at 22% = (whatever's in this bracket)

If you earn $60,000, your EFFECTIVE tax rate is about 14%, not 22%.

## Commonly Missed Deductions
- Student loan interest (up to $2,500)
- Home office deduction (if self-employed)
- Health Savings Account (HSA) contributions
- Charitable donations (even without itemizing, up to $300)
- State and local taxes (SALT, up to $10,000)
- Educator expenses (teachers get $300)
- Moving expenses (military only now)
- Energy-efficient home improvements (tax credits)

## Tax-Advantaged Accounts Cheat Sheet
| Account | Tax Benefit | 2025 Limit | Best For |
|---------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| 401(k) | Pre-tax contributions | $23,500 | Retirement, employer match |
| Roth IRA | Tax-free withdrawals | $7,000 | Retirement, young earners |
| HSA | Triple tax advantage | $4,300 single / $8,550 family | Healthcare + stealth retirement |
| 529 Plan | Tax-free growth for education | Varies by state | Saving for kids' college |
| FSA | Pre-tax for healthcare | $3,300 | Predictable medical expenses |

The HSA is the most powerful account in the tax code: deduction going in, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for medical expenses. After 65, it works like a Traditional IRA for any purpose.

---

# Insurance Essentials

## Insurance You Need by Life Stage

### Single, No Dependents (20s)
- Health insurance (mandatory)
- Renters insurance (~$15/month, covers your stuff)
- Auto insurance (required by law)
- Skip: life insurance (no one depends on your income)

### Married / Partner
- Add: Life insurance (term, 10-12x your income)
- Add: Disability insurance (protects your earning power)
- Upgrade: Auto if sharing vehicles

### With Kids
- Increase: Life insurance (both parents)
- Add: Umbrella policy ($1M+ liability protection, ~$200/year)
- Consider: 529 plan (education savings)

### Homeowner
- Homeowners insurance (required by lender)
- Flood insurance (if in flood zone, NOT covered by standard homeowners)
- Umbrella policy (definitely now)

## Term vs Whole Life Insurance
| Factor | Term Life | Whole Life |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Cost | $20-50/month | $200-500/month |
| Coverage period | 10-30 years | Lifetime |
| Cash value | None | Builds slowly |
| Best for | 99% of people | Almost nobody (see below) |
| ROI | Pure insurance, cheap | Terrible investment vehicle |

**Richy's Take**: "Buy term life insurance and invest the difference. A $500K 20-year term policy costs about $30/month for a healthy 30-year-old. Whole life for the same coverage would be $400+/month. Put that $370 difference in an index fund instead."

FILE: data/knowledge_base/05_kids_education_life_events.md  (118 lines)

# Kids & Family Financial Education

## Teaching Money by Age Group

### Ages 5-8: The Basics
- Use physical coins and bills (kids need to SEE money)
- Three jars: SPEND, SAVE, GIVE
- Play store: let them "buy" things with fake money
- Teach: "Things cost money. Money comes from work."
- Allowance: $1-3/week tied to age-appropriate chores
- Key concept: Delayed gratification ("Save 3 weeks and you can buy the bigger toy")

### Ages 9-12: Building Skills
- Open a kids savings account (watch the balance grow)
- Introduce compound interest with a simple example: "If I give you $1 that doubles every day for 30 days, you'd have over $500 million"
- Let them make purchasing decisions with their own money
- Teach comparison shopping (which cereal is the better deal per ounce?)
- Introduce earning: lawn mowing, lemonade stand, pet sitting
- Allowance: $5-10/week, require saving at least 20%
- Key concept: Opportunity cost ("If you buy this game, you can't buy those shoes")

### Ages 13-17: Real World Prep
- Open a teen checking account with debit card
- Teach them to read a bank statement
- Introduce basic investing concepts (what is a stock? what is an index fund?)
- Help them file taxes on their first job income
- Discuss wants vs needs with real examples from their life
- Introduce credit concepts (what a credit score is, why it matters)
- Let them manage a small budget for school supplies or activities
- Key concept: "Pay yourself first" and compound growth over decades

## Compound Interest for Kids (The Penny Example)
Would you rather have $1,000,000 today or a penny that doubles every day for 30 days?

| Day | Penny Option |
|-----|-------------|
| Day 1 | $0.01 |
| Day 5 | $0.16 |
| Day 10 | $5.12 |
| Day 15 | $163.84 |
| Day 20 | $5,242.88 |
| Day 25 | $167,772.16 |
| Day 30 | $5,368,709.12 |

The penny wins! This is the power of compound growth. Starting early is the biggest advantage in building wealth.

## 529 Plan Guide
- Tax-advantaged savings for education expenses
- Contributions grow tax-free
- Withdrawals are tax-free when used for qualified education expenses
- Many states offer a state tax deduction for contributions
- Can be used for K-12 tuition (up to $10K/year), college, grad school, trade schools
- If the child doesn't go to college, can transfer to another family member or roll into a Roth IRA (new rule, up to $35,000 lifetime)

---

# Life Event Financial Checklists

## First Job Checklist
- [ ] Set up direct deposit
- [ ] Enroll in 401(k) immediately (at least up to employer match)
- [ ] Choose health insurance plan (HDHP + HSA if healthy)
- [ ] Start emergency fund ($1,000 goal, then 3 months)
- [ ] Set up a budget using your actual take-home pay
- [ ] Start building credit (secured card or authorized user)
- [ ] Check tax withholding (use IRS W-4 calculator)

## Getting Married Checklist
- [ ] Discuss debts openly (both sides, full transparency)
- [ ] Decide: joint accounts, separate, or hybrid
- [ ] Update tax withholding (may file jointly now)
- [ ] Review and combine insurance policies
- [ ] Update beneficiaries on all accounts
- [ ] Set shared financial goals (house, travel, retirement)
- [ ] Discuss spending philosophies (saver vs spender)
- [ ] Create a joint budget

## Having a Baby Checklist
- [ ] Review health insurance (add baby within 30 days of birth)
- [ ] Start or increase life insurance (both parents)
- [ ] Update wills and guardianship documents
- [ ] Start a 529 plan (even small contributions grow)
- [ ] Budget for childcare costs ($800-2,500/month average)
- [ ] Maximize FSA/dependent care FSA ($5,000/year tax-free)
- [ ] Plan for parental leave income gap
- [ ] Increase emergency fund (now covering 3 people)

## Buying a Home Checklist
- [ ] Save 20% down payment (avoid PMI)
- [ ] If can't do 20%, at least 3.5-5% for FHA/conventional
- [ ] Get pre-approved BEFORE house hunting
- [ ] Total housing costs should be under 28% of gross income
- [ ] Budget for closing costs (2-5% of purchase price)
- [ ] Budget for moving + immediate repairs/furnishing
- [ ] Keep an emergency fund separate from your down payment
- [ ] Don't make major purchases or open new credit before closing
- [ ] Compare at least 3 lenders for best mortgage rate

## Job Loss Emergency Plan
- [ ] File for unemployment immediately (don't wait)
- [ ] Review severance package carefully before signing
- [ ] COBRA or marketplace health insurance (within 60 days)
- [ ] Cut all non-essential spending immediately
- [ ] Contact lenders about hardship programs (before you miss payments)
- [ ] Update resume and LinkedIn same week
- [ ] Apply to 5+ jobs per day minimum
- [ ] Do not touch retirement accounts (penalties + tax hit)
- [ ] Lean on emergency fund (this is what it's for)

## Retirement Transition Checklist
- [ ] Calculate your "number" (annual expenses × 25)
- [ ] Plan Social Security timing (waiting until 70 = 24% more)
- [ ] Set up withdrawal strategy (which accounts to draw from first)
- [ ] Convert to Roth strategically in low-income years
- [ ] Review Medicare enrollment (Part A, B, D, Medigap)
- [ ] Downsize housing if it frees up significant equity
- [ ] Review estate planning (will, trust, power of attorney)
- [ ] Plan for healthcare costs (average couple needs $315,000 for retirement healthcare)

FILE: data/knowledge_base/06_calculators_and_reference.md  (141 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Financial Calculator Formulas & Reference Data

## Core Formulas

### Compound Interest
A = P × (1 + r/n)^(n×t)
- A = final amount
- P = principal (starting amount)
- r = annual interest rate (decimal)
- n = number of times compounded per year (12 for monthly)
- t = number of years

Example: $10,000 at 7% compounded monthly for 20 years
A = 10000 × (1 + 0.07/12)^(12×20) = $40,387.39

### Future Value of Regular Contributions
FV = PMT × [((1 + r/n)^(n×t) - 1) / (r/n)]
- PMT = regular payment amount
- Example: $500/month at 10% for 30 years = $1,130,243

### Debt Payoff Calculator
Months to payoff = -log(1 - (balance × rate/12) / payment) / log(1 + rate/12)
Total interest = (monthly_payment × months) - original_balance

### Mortgage Affordability
Maximum monthly housing payment = Gross monthly income × 0.28
Maximum total debt payments = Gross monthly income × 0.36

### Monthly Mortgage Payment
M = P × [r(1+r)^n] / [(1+r)^n - 1]
- M = monthly payment
- P = loan principal
- r = monthly interest rate (annual rate / 12)
- n = total number of payments (years × 12)

### Emergency Fund Target
Target = Monthly essential expenses × Coverage months
Essential expenses = Rent + Utilities + Food + Insurance + Transportation + Min debt payments

### Retirement Number (4% Rule)
Amount needed = Annual retirement expenses × 25
Example: Need $50,000/year → Need $1,250,000 saved

### Credit Utilization
Utilization = (Total balances / Total credit limits) × 100
Target: Under 30%, ideal under 10%

### Debt-to-Income Ratio
DTI = (Total monthly debt payments / Gross monthly income) × 100
Target: Under 36%, mortgage approval typically needs under 43%

### Investment Growth (Rule of 72)
Years to double = 72 / Annual return rate
At 7% return: 72/7 = ~10.3 years to double
At 10% return: 72/10 = ~7.2 years to double

### Inflation-Adjusted Return
Real return = ((1 + nominal return) / (1 + inflation rate)) - 1
If stocks return 10% and inflation is 3%: real return = 6.8%

### Break-Even on Refinancing
Months to break even = Total closing costs / Monthly payment savings
If closing costs = $4,000 and you save $150/month: break even in 27 months

## Reference Data Tables

### 2025 Federal Tax Brackets (Single Filer)
| Taxable Income | Rate |
|---------------|------|
| $0 - $11,600 | 10% |
| $11,601 - $47,150 | 12% |
| $47,151 - $100,525 | 22% |
| $100,526 - $191,950 | 24% |

| $243,726 - $609,350 | 35% |
| Over $609,350 | 37% |
| Standard Deduction | $15,000 |

FILE: data/knowledge_base/budgeting_methods.md  (50 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Budgeting Methods

## The 50/30/20 Rule
Split after-tax (take-home) income into three categories:
- **50% Needs:** Housing, groceries, utilities, insurance, minimum debt payments, transportation
- **30% Wants:** Dining out, entertainment, subscriptions, shopping, travel, hobbies
- **20% Savings & Debt Payoff:** Emergency fund, retirement contributions, extra debt payments, investments

### Example on $5,000/month take-home:
- Needs: $2,500
- Wants: $1,500
- Savings: $1,000

If your needs exceed 50%, focus on reducing your largest fixed expenses (housing and transportation).

## Zero-Based Budget
Every dollar gets assigned a job. Income minus all categories equals exactly zero.
- Best for people who want detailed control
- Can use apps like YNAB (You Need A Budget) or EveryDollar

## Pay Yourself First
1. Set up automatic transfers on payday to savings and investment accounts
2. Pay all bills
3. Whatever is left is your spending money
- Best for people who hate tracking every purchase

## Envelope Method
- Withdraw cash for variable spending categories
- Put cash in physical (or digital) envelopes: Groceries, Dining, Entertainment, etc.
- When an envelope is empty, stop spending in that category
- Best for overspenders who need tangible limits

## Common Budget Mistakes
1. **Not tracking subscriptions** — Average American spends $219/month on subscriptions
2. **Forgetting irregular expenses** — Car maintenance, gifts, annual fees
3. **Being too restrictive** — Budgets without fun money fail. Include a guilt-free category.
4. **Not adjusting** — Review and adjust monthly for the first 3 months

## Budget Percentages by Category (Recommended)
- Housing: 25-30%
- Transportation: 10-15%
- Food (groceries + dining): 10-15%
- Insurance: 10-15%
- Savings/Investments: 15-20%
- Utilities: 5-10%
- Entertainment: 5-10%
- Personal/Misc: 5-10%

## High-Yield Savings for Budget Goals
Keep your emergency fund and short-term savings in a high-yield savings account earning 4-5% APY (2025 rates). Top options: Marcus by Goldman Sachs, Ally Bank, Wealthfront, SoFi.
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/credit_scores.md  (59 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Credit Scores and Reports

## Understanding Your Credit Score

### FICO Score Ranges (300-850)
| Score | Rating | Population % | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 800-850 | Exceptional | ~21% | Best rates on everything |
| 740-799 | Very Good | ~25% | Near-best rates |
| 670-739 | Good | ~21% | Average rates |
| 580-669 | Fair | ~17% | Subprime rates, higher costs |
| 300-579 | Poor | ~16% | May be denied credit |

### The 5 Factors
1. **Payment History (35%)** — Most important. Even one 30-day late payment can drop your score 60-110 points. Set up autopay for minimums on everything.
2. **Credit Utilization (30%)** — Balance ÷ credit limit. Keep under 30%, ideally under 10%. Applies to each card AND overall. Pay before statement closing date to lower reported utilization.
3. **Length of Credit History (15%)** — Average age of all accounts. Never close your oldest credit card. If you have no credit history, become an authorized user on a family member's old card.
4. **Credit Mix (10%)** — Having different types (credit card, auto loan, mortgage, student loan) helps slightly.
5. **New Inquiries (10%)** — Each hard inquiry costs 5-10 points temporarily. Multiple inquiries for same type (mortgage shopping) within 14-45 days count as one.

## Building Credit from Scratch

### Step 1: Get Your First Credit Card
- Secured credit card (requires deposit): Discover It Secured, Capital One Platinum Secured
- Student credit card if in college
- Become authorized user on parent's/spouse's card with good history

### Step 2: Use It Responsibly
- Use for ONE small recurring purchase (gas, streaming subscription)
- Pay balance in FULL every month (before the due date)
- Never use more than 30% of your limit

### Step 3: Wait and Build
- After 6 months, you'll have a scoreable credit file
- After 12-24 months, apply for a better card

## Quick Ways to Improve Your Score
1. **Pay down credit cards** to under 30% utilization (fastest impact: 1-2 months)
2. **Get added as authorized user** on someone's old, low-utilization card
3. **Dispute errors** on credit report (30% of reports contain errors)
4. **Request credit limit increase** (lowers utilization ratio without paying anything)
5. **Keep old accounts open** even if you don't use them
6. **Use Experian Boost** (free, adds utility/phone payment history)

## Checking Your Credit
- **Free credit reports:** AnnualCreditReport.com (free weekly from all 3 bureaus)
- **Free credit scores:** Credit Karma, Discover, many bank apps
- Checking your own credit is a "soft inquiry" — it never hurts your score

## The Cost of Bad Credit
On a $300,000 30-year mortgage:
- 6.5% rate (excellent credit): $1,896/month — $382,633 total interest
- 8.0% rate (poor credit): $2,201/month — $492,467 total interest
- **Difference: $109,834** more in interest over the life of the loan

## Protecting Your Credit
- Freeze credit at all 3 bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) — free, prevents unauthorized accounts
- Monitor for suspicious activity monthly
- Never share credit card numbers over unsecured channels
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/debt_management.md  (63 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Debt Management

## Types of Debt

### Good Debt (Low Interest, Builds Value)
- Mortgage (typically 6-7.5% in 2025)
- Student loans (federal: 5.5% for undergrad, 7% for grad in 2025)
- Business loans for proven concepts

### Bad Debt (High Interest, No Asset)
- Credit cards (average APR: 22-28% in 2025)
- Payday loans (300-500%+ APR — avoid at all costs)
- Personal loans for consumption (8-36% APR)
- Buy Now Pay Later with missed payments

## Debt Payoff Strategies

### The Avalanche Method (Mathematically Optimal)
Pay minimum on everything, throw all extra at the HIGHEST interest rate debt.
- Saves the most money in total interest
- Can feel slow if your highest-rate debt is also your largest balance
- Best for disciplined, numbers-driven people

### The Snowball Method (Psychologically Powerful)
Pay minimum on everything, throw all extra at the SMALLEST balance debt.
- Quick wins build motivation
- You may pay more total interest
- 70% completion rate vs ~50% for avalanche
- Best for people who need motivation wins

### Debt Consolidation
Combine multiple debts into one payment, ideally at a lower interest rate.
- Balance transfer cards: 0% APR for 12-21 months (3-5% transfer fee)
- Personal consolidation loan: Usually 8-15% APR
- Only works if you DON'T rack up new debt on the freed-up cards

## Debt Priority Ladder
1. Make minimum payments on ALL debts (always)
2. Get employer 401(k) match (free money)
3. Pay off high-interest debt (>7% APR) aggressively
4. Build emergency fund to 3-6 months
5. Pay medium-interest debt (5-7%) OR invest (roughly equal)
6. Low-interest debt (< 5%) can coexist with investing

## Key Numbers
- Total U.S. household debt (2025): $17.9 trillion
- Average credit card balance: $6,501
- Average student loan balance: $37,338
- Average car loan: $23,792

## Debt-to-Income Ratio
- Monthly debt payments ÷ gross monthly income × 100
- Under 20%: Healthy
- 20-35%: Manageable
- 36-49%: Struggling
- 50%+: Crisis — seek help

## When to Seek Professional Help
- Debt-to-income ratio over 50%
- Considering bankruptcy
- Being sued by creditors
- Unable to make minimum payments
Contact: National Foundation for Credit Counseling (NFCC.org) for free or low-cost counseling.
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/emergency_funds.md  (53 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Emergency Funds

## What Is an Emergency Fund?
Cash reserves set aside for unexpected expenses — job loss, medical bills, car repairs, home emergencies. This is your financial safety net.

## How Much Do You Need?

### The Standard Rule: 3-6 Months of Essential Expenses
Essential expenses include: rent/mortgage, groceries, utilities, insurance, transportation, minimum debt payments.

### Your Number Depends On:
- **3 months** if: Dual income household, stable job, no dependents
- **6 months** if: Single income, variable income, have dependents, self-employed
- **9-12 months** if: Freelancer, seasonal worker, commission-based income, single parent

### Quick Calculation
Monthly essentials × months of coverage = emergency fund target.
Example: $3,000/month × 6 months = $18,000 target.

## Building Your Emergency Fund

### Starting from Zero
1. **First milestone: $1,000** — Covers most small emergencies (car repair, medical copay, appliance replacement)
2. **Second milestone: 1 month of expenses** — Buys time if income drops
3. **Third milestone: 3 months** — Handles job loss in strong job market
4. **Final target: 6 months** — Full financial security

### Strategies to Build Faster
- **Automate first:** Set up auto-transfer on payday, even $25/week
- **Sell unused items:** Declutter and sell on Facebook Marketplace, eBay
- **Side hustle:** Dedicate 100% of side income to emergency fund until funded
- **Tax refund:** Direct your refund straight to savings
- **Round up:** Use apps like Acorns or bank round-up features

## Where to Keep Your Emergency Fund
- **High-Yield Savings Account (HYSA):** Best option. Earns 4-5% APY (2025), FDIC insured, accessible in 1-2 days.
- **Money Market Account:** Similar yields, may come with check-writing ability.
- **NOT stocks or investments:** Emergency fund must be stable and liquid. Markets can drop 30%+ when you need the money most (recessions often cause both job loss AND market crashes).
- **NOT your checking account:** Too tempting to spend. Keep it separate.

## When to Use Your Emergency Fund
**YES:** Job loss, medical emergency, essential car/home repair, urgent travel for family emergency.
**NO:** Sales, vacations, "good deals," planned expenses, wants.

## After Using Your Emergency Fund
Replenish it as your #1 financial priority before resuming extra investing or discretionary spending.

## Emergency Fund vs. Other Savings
| Type | Purpose | Where to Keep | How Much |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emergency Fund | Unexpected crises | HYSA | 3-6 months expenses |
| Sinking Fund | Planned irregular expenses | HYSA | Varies by goal |
| Investment Account | Long-term wealth | Brokerage | As much as possible |
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/insurance_essentials.md  (61 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Insurance Essentials

## Why Insurance Matters
Insurance protects you from financial catastrophe. The rule: insure against things that would **financially devastate** you. Don't insure small, affordable losses.

## Types of Insurance Everyone Needs

### Health Insurance
- **Average premium (2025):** $477/month individual (employer-sponsored: ~$138 after employer contribution)
- **Key terms:** Premium (monthly cost), deductible (you pay first), copay (fixed per visit), out-of-pocket max (your annual ceiling)
- **HSA-eligible plans (HDHP):** Higher deductible, lower premium, can contribute to HSA for triple tax benefit
- **ACA Marketplace:** Open enrollment Nov 1 – Jan 15. Subsidies available based on income.

### Auto Insurance
- **Required:** Liability coverage (minimum varies by state)
- **Recommended:** Comprehensive + collision if car worth > $10,000
- **Ways to save:** Raise deductible to $1,000, bundle with home insurance, maintain good credit, shop around yearly
- **Average cost (2025):** $2,150/year for full coverage

### Renters Insurance
- **Average cost:** $15-25/month
- **Covers:** Personal belongings (theft, fire, water damage), liability if someone is injured in your apartment
- **Often overlooked** but incredibly valuable. Your landlord's insurance does NOT cover your stuff.

### Homeowners Insurance
- **Average cost (2025):** $2,100/year
- **Covers:** Structure, personal property, liability, additional living expenses
- **Tips:** Review annually, increase coverage as home value rises, document belongings with photos/video
- **Not covered:** Floods (need separate NFIP policy), earthquakes (separate policy in affected areas)

### Life Insurance
- **Who needs it:** Anyone with dependents (spouse, kids, aging parents you support)
- **Type to buy:** Term life insurance (20-30 year term, pure coverage, cheap)
- **How much:** 10-15× your annual income
- **Example:** 30-year-old, healthy, $500K 20-year term: ~$25-35/month
- **Skip:** Whole life insurance (expensive, bad investment returns — invest the savings difference instead)

### Disability Insurance
- **Most overlooked insurance.** You're more likely to become disabled than die before 65.
- **Short-term disability:** Covers 60-70% of income for 3-6 months
- **Long-term disability:** Covers 50-60% of income for years/decades
- **Check your employer benefits first** — many include basic coverage

### Umbrella Insurance
- **Extra liability coverage** beyond auto and home limits
- **Who needs it:** Net worth over $300,000, rental property owners, anyone at risk of lawsuit
- **Cost:** ~$150-300/year for $1 million coverage — incredible value

## Insurance Rules of Thumb
1. **Raise deductibles** to lower premiums (but keep enough cash for the deductible)
2. **Bundle policies** for 10-25% discount
3. **Shop around every 2-3 years** — loyalty penalties are real
4. **Never insure what you can afford to replace** (skip extended warranties, phone insurance)
5. **Review coverage annually** as life changes (marriage, kids, home purchase)

## Insurance Scams to Avoid
- Whole life insurance sold as an "investment"
- Credit card protection insurance
- Extended warranties on electronics
- Single-disease policies (cancer insurance, etc.)
- Mortgage life insurance (get term life instead, it's cheaper and more flexible)
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/investing_basics.md  (51 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Investing Basics

## What Is Investing?
Investing means putting your money to work so it grows over time. Instead of keeping cash under your mattress (where inflation eats its value), you buy assets — stocks, bonds, real estate, or funds — that historically increase in value.

## Key Concepts

### Stocks
A stock is a tiny ownership stake in a company. When the company profits, your share becomes worth more. Historically, the U.S. stock market returns about 10% per year on average (before inflation). Individual years can range from -37% to +52%.

### Bonds
A bond is a loan you make to a government or corporation. They pay you fixed interest and return your principal at maturity. Bonds are less volatile than stocks but offer lower returns (typically 4-6%).

### Index Funds
An index fund tracks an entire market. The S&P 500 index fund owns all 500 of the largest U.S. companies. Index funds charge very low fees (0.03-0.20%) and outperform 90% of professional fund managers over 15+ years.

### ETFs vs Mutual Funds
ETFs trade like stocks throughout the day. Mutual funds trade once per day at market close. ETFs typically have lower expense ratios and better tax efficiency. Both are excellent vehicles for diversified investing.

## Getting Started

### 1. Open a Brokerage Account
Top brokerages for beginners: Fidelity, Charles Schwab, Vanguard. All offer zero-commission trades and no account minimums.

### 2. Start with Index Funds
- **VTI** or **VTSAX** — Total U.S. stock market
- **VOO** or **FXAIX** — S&P 500
- **VXUS** — International stocks
- **BND** — Total bond market

### 3. Dollar-Cost Average
Invest the same amount on a regular schedule regardless of market conditions. This removes emotion and reduces timing risk.

### 4. Diversify
Spread investments across asset classes, sectors, and geographies. A simple 3-fund portfolio (U.S. stocks + international stocks + bonds) covers the entire global market.

## Risk and Return
Higher potential returns always come with higher risk. Stocks return more than bonds but are more volatile. Your risk tolerance depends on your time horizon — money needed in 1-3 years belongs in a savings account, not the stock market.

## Compound Growth
$10,000 invested at 10% annual return:
- Year 10: $25,937
- Year 20: $67,275
- Year 30: $174,494

Adding $500/month:
- Year 10: $127,809
- Year 20: $386,968
- Year 30: $1,085,661

Time is the most powerful factor. Starting 10 years earlier matters more than doubling your monthly contribution.
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/kids_finance.md  (99 lines)

# Financial Literacy for Kids and Teens

## Ages 3-5: Money Is Real

### Key Lessons
- Coins and bills have different values
- Things cost money — you can't just take things from stores
- Sometimes you have to wait to get what you want (delayed gratification)

### Activities
- Play store with real coins
- Let them hand money to cashier
- "Do you want this small toy now, or save for the bigger one?"

## Ages 6-10: Earning, Saving, Spending

### Key Lessons
- Money comes from work (not ATMs or phones)
- Three jars: Save, Spend, Share
- Needs vs Wants (food = need, toy = want)
- Setting savings goals

### Activities
- Earn money through age-appropriate chores
- Use clear jars so they SEE money growing
- Match their savings (Bank of Mom/Dad pays interest)
- Let them make spending mistakes with their own money — priceless learning

### Allowance Tips
- Tie some money to chores, give some unconditionally
- Start with age-appropriate amount ($0.50-$1 per year of age per week)
- Don't bail them out when they spend it all early

## Ages 11-14: Compound Interest and Entrepreneurship

### Key Lessons
- How compound interest works (use concrete examples)
- Basics of budgeting (income - expenses = savings)
- How banks work
- Inflation: why $10 today ≠ $10 in 20 years
- Introduction to entrepreneurship

### Activities
- Open a youth savings account (many banks allow with parent)
- Teach them to track spending for one month
- Start a micro-business: lawn care, pet sitting, crafts, tutoring
- Play stock market simulator games
- Show them YOUR budget (age-appropriate transparency)

### The Compound Interest Story
If a 12-year-old invests $50/month starting at 15 until age 65:
- Total invested: $30,000
- Value at 65 (8% return): ~$349,000
If they wait until 25: $30,000 invested → ~$149,000 (less than half!)

## Ages 15-18: Real World Prep

### Key Lessons
- How taxes work (they'll see it on first paycheck!)
- Credit and credit scores (NOT debt = normal)
- Compound interest on debt (credit cards)
- Basic investing (index funds, not day trading)
- Needs vs wants at a higher level (new car vs used)
- How student loans work

### Activities
- Get a part-time job
- Open a Roth IRA (if they have earned income) — best head start ever
- Get a secured credit card and learn responsible use
- Review college cost calculators
- Practice negotiating (cell phone bill, subscription prices)
- Create a personal budget

### First Paycheck Lesson
Show them the difference between gross and net pay. Explain each deduction:
- Federal income tax
- State income tax (if applicable)
- Social Security (6.2%)
- Medicare (1.45%)

## Money Conversations to Have at Every Age
1. "In our family, we choose to spend on X because Y" (values-based spending)
2. "We can't afford that right now" → Better: "That's not in our plan right now"
3. "What do you think that costs?" (develop price awareness)
4. "How could you earn money for that?" (entrepreneurial thinking)
5. "What would happen if you saved that instead?" (opportunity cost)

## Common Mistakes Parents Make
1. Never talking about money (kids learn silence = shame)
2. Always saying yes (kids don't learn delayed gratification)
3. Not letting kids fail with money (small failures now prevent big failures later)
4. Teaching fear of money instead of respect for it
5. Not modeling good behavior (kids watch what you DO, not what you say)

## Resources for Kids
- Books: "The Berenstain Bears' Trouble with Money", "If You Made a Million"
- Games: Monopoly, The Game of Life, Cashflow for Kids
- Apps: Greenlight (kid debit card with savings features), FamZoo, GoHenry
- Agent Richy's Kids Education module with video lessons and quizzes!
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/real_estate.md  (86 lines)

# Real Estate and Mortgages

## Home Buying Basics

### The 28/36 Rule
- **28% rule:** Monthly housing costs (mortgage + tax + insurance) should be ≤ 28% of gross monthly income
- **36% rule:** Total debt payments should be ≤ 36% of gross monthly income
- Example on $100K salary ($8,333/month): Max housing = $2,333, max total debt = $3,000

### Down Payment
- **Conventional loan:** 5-20% down. Under 20% requires PMI ($100-300/month on $300K home)
- **FHA loan:** 3.5% down (lower credit score OK, 580+)
- **VA loan:** 0% down (veterans/active military)
- **USDA loan:** 0% down (rural areas, income limits)
- **Saving 20% down on $350K home = $70,000** — takes average buyer 5-7 years

### Current Mortgage Rates (2025)
- 30-year fixed: approximately 6.5-7.5%
- 15-year fixed: approximately 5.5-6.5%
- ARM (adjustable): Lower initial rate, adjusts after 5-7 years

### Closing Costs
- Typically 2-5% of purchase price
- On $350K home: $7,000-17,500
- Includes: appraisal, title insurance, attorney fees, origination fees, prepaid taxes and insurance

## Mortgage Math

### Monthly Payment (30-Year Fixed)
At 7% rate:
| Home Price | Down 20% | Monthly P&I | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $250,000 | $50,000 | $1,331 | $479,017 |
| $350,000 | $70,000 | $1,863 | $670,824 |
| $450,000 | $90,000 | $2,395 | $862,631 |
| $550,000 | $110,000 | $2,928 | $1,054,437 |

Add 30-40% for taxes, insurance, maintenance for true monthly cost.

### 15-Year vs 30-Year
$300K loan at 7% (30-year) vs 6.5% (15-year):
- 30-year: $1,996/month, $418,527 total interest
- 15-year: $2,613/month, $170,362 total interest
- **Savings: $248,165** — but $617/month more

### Refinancing
Worth considering when you can drop rate by 0.75-1%+ and plan to stay 3+ years. Break-even: closing costs ÷ monthly savings = months to recoup.

## Rent vs Buy Decision

### Price-to-Rent Ratio
Home price ÷ annual rent
- Under 15: Buying favored
- 15-20: Roughly equal
- Over 20: Renting may be better

### The 5-Year Rule
Plan to stay at least 5 years to make buying worthwhile after transaction costs (buying costs + selling costs = ~10% of home value).

### Hidden Costs of Ownership
- Property taxes: 0.5-2.5% of home value annually
- Maintenance: 1-2% of home value annually
- Homeowners insurance: $1,500-3,000/year
- HOA fees: $0-$500+/month
- Capital improvements: New roof ($10-25K), HVAC ($5-10K), water heater ($1-3K)

## Real Estate as Investment

### Primary Residence
- Builds equity over time
- Tax benefits: Mortgage interest deduction (if itemizing), up to $250K ($500K married) capital gains exclusion
- Historical appreciation: ~3-5% annually (varies widely by area)
- NOT purely an investment — it's a place to live that may appreciate

### Rental Properties
- Cap rate (net operating income ÷ purchase price) should be > 5%
- Budget for vacancies (8-10% of rental income), maintenance, property management
- Leverage amplifies returns but also risk
- Not passive income without a property manager

## First-Time Buyer Tips
1. Get pre-approved BEFORE house hunting
2. Don't skip the home inspection ($300-500, can save you $50,000+)
3. Budget for unexpected repairs (set aside 1% of home value/year day one)
4. Don't buy the most expensive home you qualify for — that's the bank's max, not yours
5. Consider total commute cost — a cheaper house 30 miles out may cost more in gas and time
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/retirement_planning.md  (58 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Retirement Planning

## How Much Do You Need?

### The 25x Rule
Multiply your desired annual retirement spending by 25. This is based on the 4% safe withdrawal rate.
- $40,000/year lifestyle → $1,000,000 needed
- $60,000/year lifestyle → $1,500,000 needed
- $80,000/year lifestyle → $2,000,000 needed
- $100,000/year lifestyle → $2,500,000 needed

### The 4% Rule
You can withdraw 4% of your portfolio in year one of retirement, then adjust for inflation each year. Historically, this has sustained portfolios for 30+ years in 95% of scenarios.

## Retirement Accounts (2025 Limits)

### 401(k)
- **Employee contribution limit:** $23,500/year
- **Catch-up (age 50+):** additional $7,500 ($31,000 total)
- **Super catch-up (ages 60-63):** additional $11,250 ($34,750 total) — new for 2025
- **Total 401(k) limit (including employer):** $70,000
- Traditional 401(k): pre-tax, reduces taxable income now
- Roth 401(k): after-tax, withdrawals are tax-free in retirement

### IRA (Individual Retirement Account)
- **Contribution limit:** $7,000/year ($8,000 if 50+)
- **Roth IRA income limits:** Phase-out begins at $150,000 (single) / $236,000 (married filing jointly)
- **Traditional IRA:** Deductible if no workplace plan or income below limits
- **Backdoor Roth:** High earners can convert traditional IRA contributions to Roth

### HSA (Health Savings Account)
- **2025 limits:** $4,300 (individual) / $8,550 (family)
- Triple tax advantage: deductible going in, grows tax-free, withdrawals tax-free for medical expenses
- After 65, can withdraw for any purpose (taxed like traditional IRA)
- Best strategy: pay medical expenses out of pocket now, let HSA grow, withdraw tax-free later

## Savings Timeline

### Starting at Age 25 (Target: $1.5M by 65)
- Need ~$350/month at 8% average return
- Total contributed: $168,000 → Grows to $1,500,000

### Starting at Age 35
- Need ~$800/month at 8%
- Total contributed: $288,000 → Grows to $1,500,000

### Starting at Age 45
- Need ~$2,000/month at 8%
- Total contributed: $480,000 → Grows to $1,500,000

The cost of waiting 10 years: you contribute MORE money and end up with LESS. Start now.

## Social Security
- Full retirement age: 67 (for those born 1960+)
- Early: age 62 (reduced ~30%)
- Delayed: up to age 70 (increased ~24%)
- Average monthly benefit (2025): ~$1,976
- Do NOT rely on Social Security alone — it's designed to replace only about 40% of pre-retirement income
================================================================================

FILE: data/knowledge_base/side_hustles.md  (74 lines)

# Side Hustles and Extra Income

## Why Extra Income Matters
The average American household spends $72,967/year (2024 BLS). Side income can:
- Accelerate emergency fund building
- Pay off debt 2-3x faster
- Fund Roth IRA without touching primary income
- Build skills and career safety net

## Best Side Hustles by Skill Level

### Low Barrier (Start This Week)
| Hustle | Typical Earnings | Time Required |
|---|---|---|
| Rideshare (Uber/Lyft) | $15-30/hour | Flexible |
| Food delivery (DoorDash) | $12-25/hour | Flexible |
| Pet sitting (Rover) | $20-50/night | Evenings/weekends |
| Tasking (TaskRabbit) | $20-40/hour | Flexible |
| Selling items (eBay/FB Marketplace) | Varies | A few hours/week |

### Medium Skill (Learn in 1-3 Months)
| Hustle | Typical Earnings | Time to Learn |
|---|---|---|
| Freelance writing | $30-75/hour | 1-2 months |
| Virtual assistant | $20-40/hour | 2-4 weeks |
| Bookkeeping | $25-50/hour | 2-3 months |
| Social media management | $25-75/hour | 1-2 months |
| Tutoring | $20-60/hour | Minimal if expert |

### High Skill (Higher Earning Potential)
| Hustle | Typical Earnings | Time to Learn |
|---|---|---|
| Web development | $50-150/hour | 3-6 months |
| Graphic design | $40-100/hour | 3-6 months |
| Consulting (your industry) | $75-300/hour | Already have skills |
| Online course creation | $1,000-50,000+ | 2-3 months |
| App development | $50-200/hour | 6-12 months |

## Side Hustle Tax Rules
- Side income over $400/year requires filing Schedule SE (self-employment tax: 15.3%)
- Track ALL expenses for deductions: mileage ($0.70/mile in 2025), home office, supplies, software
- Set aside 25-30% of side income for taxes
- Consider quarterly estimated tax payments to avoid penalties

## From Side Hustle to Business
1. Validate the idea (make $1,000 first)
2. Track income and expenses from day one
3. Open a separate bank account
4. Get proper insurance if needed
5. Consider LLC for liability protection
6. Scale by raising prices before adding hours

## Kid and Teen Side Hustles

### Ages 8-12
- Lemonade stand / bake sales
- Lawn care (with parent supervision)
- Dog walking in neighborhood
- Selling crafts or artwork

### Ages 13-17
- Babysitting ($10-20/hour)
- Social media for local businesses
- Tutoring younger students
- YouTube/content creation
- Car detailing
- Holiday decoration setup/removal
- Tech support for neighbors

## Financial Rules for Side Income
1. **100% to debt** if you have high-interest debt
2. **100% to emergency fund** until it's fully funded
3. **After that:** 50% investing, 30% fun, 20% skill building
4. **Never increase lifestyle** based on side hustle income (unless it's consistent 6+ months)

FILE: data/knowledge_base/tax_planning.md  (61 lines)
----------------------------------------
# Tax Planning Basics

## 2025 Federal Income Tax Brackets (Single Filers)

| Taxable Income | Rate |
|---|---|
| $0 – $11,925 | 10% |
| $11,926 – $48,475 | 12% |
| $48,476 – $103,350 | 22% |
| $103,351 – $197,300 | 24% |
| $197,301 – $250,525 | 32% |
| $250,526 – $626,350 | 35% |
| $626,351+ | 37% |

## 2025 Standard Deduction
- Single: $15,000
- Married Filing Jointly: $30,000
- Head of Household: $22,500
- Age 65+ additional: $2,000 (single) / $1,600 (married)

## Key Tax-Saving Strategies

### 1. Maximize Pre-Tax Retirement Contributions
- 401(k): $23,500 contribution = ~$5,875 tax savings (in 25% bracket)
- Traditional IRA: $7,000 deduction if eligible
- HSA: $4,300 individual / $8,550 family

### 2. Tax-Loss Harvesting
Sell investments at a loss to offset gains. Net losses up to $3,000/year can offset ordinary income. Unused losses carry forward forever.

### 3. Capital Gains Tax Rates (2025)
- Short-term (held < 1 year): Taxed as ordinary income
- Long-term (held > 1 year): 0% if income under $47,025 (single), 15% up to $518,900, 20% above

### 4. HSA Triple Tax Advantage
- Contributions are tax-deductible
- Investments grow tax-free
- Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free
- After 65, HSA becomes like a traditional IRA for any purpose

### 5. Charitable Giving Strategies
- Donate appreciated stock (skip capital gains, deduct full market value)
- Donor-advised fund: Front-load contributions for big deduction year
- Qualified Charitable Distribution from IRA (age 70½+)

### 6. Roth Conversions
Convert traditional IRA/401(k) to Roth in low-income years. Pay tax now, never again. Especially valuable in sabbatical years, early retirement, or between jobs.

## Common Tax Mistakes
1. Not adjusting withholding (getting huge refunds = free loan to government)
2. Missing the April 15 deadline (or extension date October 15)
3. Not keeping receipts for deductible expenses
4. Forgetting state taxes when planning
5. Not contributing to tax-advantaged accounts

## Self-Employment Taxes
- Self-employment tax: 15.3% (12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare) on first $176,100
- Above $176,100: only 2.9% Medicare
- Deductions: Home office, mileage ($0.70/mile in 2025), health insurance, retirement contributions (SEP IRA up to $70,000)

*Note: Tax laws change frequently. Consult a CPA or tax professional for your specific situation.*
================================================================================

FILE: pages/1_Dashboard.py  (331 lines)
----------------------------------------
"""Dashboard — Professional financial overview with charts and metrics."""

import streamlit as st
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from styles import inject_styles
from config import COLORS, PLOTLY_LAYOUT, PLOTLY_COLORS, AGENTS
from utils.session import init_session_state, get_profile
from utils.calculations import (
    compound_growth, debt_payoff_schedule, savings_rate_pct,
    emergency_fund_months,
)
from components.metric_card import render_metric_row

st.set_page_config(page_title="Dashboard — Agent Richy", page_icon="📊", layout="wide")
inject_styles()
init_session_state()

# ── Session guard ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if not st.session_state.get("onboarded"):
    st.warning("Please complete onboarding first.")
    st.page_link("app.py", label="← Go to Home", use_container_width=True)
    st.stop()

profile = get_profile()
plan = st.session_state.get("financial_plan", {})

# ── Sidebar ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
with st.sidebar:
    st.markdown(f"""
    <div style="text-align: center; padding: 1rem 0;">
        <div style="font-size: 2rem;">💰</div>
        <div style="font-weight: 700; color: {COLORS['gold']};">Agent Richy</div>
    </div>
    """, unsafe_allow_html=True)
    st.markdown("---")
    st.markdown(f"**{profile.name}** · {profile.age}yr")
    st.page_link("app.py", label="🏠 Home", use_container_width=True)
    st.page_link("pages/2_Chat.py", label="💬 Chat", use_container_width=True)
    st.page_link("pages/3_Kids_Education.py", label="🎓 Kids", use_container_width=True)
    st.page_link("pages/4_My_Plan.py", label="📋 Plan", use_container_width=True)
    st.page_link("pages/5_Financial_Profile.py", label="👤 Profile", use_container_width=True)
    st.page_link("pages/6_Upgrade.py", label="⭐ Premium", use_container_width=True)

# ── Header ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
st.markdown(f"## 📊 {profile.name}'s Financial Dashboard")
st.caption("Real-time overview of your financial health. Keep chatting with agents to update your data.")

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  TOP SECTION — Key Metrics
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
income = profile.monthly_income
expenses = profile.monthly_expenses
surplus = income - expenses if income > 0 else 0
sr = savings_rate_pct(income, expenses)
ef_months = emergency_fund_months(
    profile.emergency_fund or profile.savings_balance, expenses
)
total_debt = profile.total_debt()

metrics = []
metrics.append({
    "label": "Net Worth (Est.)",
    "value": f"${(profile.savings_balance + profile.emergency_fund - total_debt):,.0f}"
             if income > 0 else "—",
    "icon": "💎",
    "delta": "",
    "delta_positive": True,
})
metrics.append({
    "label": "Monthly Savings Rate",
    "value": f"{sr:.1f}%" if income > 0 else "—",
    "icon": "📈",
    "delta": "Target: 20%",
    "delta_positive": sr >= 20,
})
metrics.append({
    "label": "Debt-to-Income",
    "value": f"{profile.debt_to_income_ratio():.0f}%"
             if income > 0 and total_debt > 0 else "0%",
    "icon": "⚖️",
    "delta": "< 36% is healthy",
    "delta_positive": profile.debt_to_income_ratio() < 36,
})
metrics.append({
    "label": "Emergency Fund",
    "value": f"{ef_months:.1f} months" if ef_months > 0 else "—",
    "icon": "🛡️",
    "delta": "Target: 6 months",
    "delta_positive": ef_months >= 3,
})

render_metric_row(metrics)

st.markdown("---")

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  MIDDLE SECTION — Charts
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
if income > 0:
    chart1, chart2 = st.columns(2)

    # ── Spending Breakdown (Donut) ────────────────────────────────────────
    with chart1:
        st.markdown("### 📊 Budget Breakdown")
        budget = plan.get("budget", {})
        needs = budget.get("needs", income * 0.50)
        wants = budget.get("wants", income * 0.30)
        save_amt = budget.get("savings", income * 0.20)

        fig = go.Figure(data=[go.Pie(
            labels=["Needs", "Wants", "Savings & Investing"],
            values=[needs, wants, save_amt],
            marker_colors=[COLORS["blue"], COLORS["gold"], COLORS["green"]],
            hole=0.55,
            textinfo="label+percent",
            textfont=dict(color="white", size=13),
        )])
        fig.update_layout(
            **PLOTLY_LAYOUT,
            height=350,
            showlegend=True,
            legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=-0.15, x=0.5, xanchor="center"),
        )
        st.plotly_chart(fig, use_container_width=True)

    # ── Income vs Expenses (Bar) ─────────────────────────────────────────
    with chart2:
        st.markdown("### 💵 Income vs Expenses")
        fig2 = go.Figure()
        fig2.add_trace(go.Bar(
            x=["Income", "Expenses", "Surplus"],
            y=[income, expenses, max(0, surplus)],
            marker_color=[COLORS["green"], COLORS["red"], COLORS["blue"]],
            text=[f"${income:,.0f}", f"${expenses:,.0f}", f"${max(0,surplus):,.0f}"],
            textposition="outside",
            textfont=dict(color="white"),
        ))
        fig2.update_layout(
            **PLOTLY_LAYOUT,
            height=350,
            showlegend=False,
            yaxis=dict(title="$/month", gridcolor=COLORS["border"]),
            xaxis=dict(gridcolor=COLORS["border"]),
        )
        st.plotly_chart(fig2, use_container_width=True)

    st.markdown("---")

    chart3, chart4 = st.columns(2)

    # ── Debt Payoff Projection ───────────────────────────────────────────
    with chart3:
        if profile.debts and surplus > 0:
            st.markdown("### 💳 Debt Payoff Projection")
            # Use highest-interest debt
            sorted_debts = sorted(
                profile.debts.items(),
                key=lambda x: profile.debt_interest_rates.get(x[0], 0),
                reverse=True,
            )
            first_name, first_bal = sorted_debts[0]
            first_rate = profile.debt_interest_rates.get(first_name, 0.18)
            min_pay = max(first_bal * 0.02, 25)
            total_pay = min_pay + (surplus * 0.5)

            if total_pay > first_bal * (first_rate / 12) and first_rate > 0:
                schedule = debt_payoff_schedule(first_bal, first_rate, total_pay)
                if schedule:
                    months_list = [s["month"] for s in schedule]
                    balances = [s["remaining"] for s in schedule]
                    fig3 = go.Figure()
                    fig3.add_trace(go.Scatter(
                        x=months_list, y=balances,
                        fill="tozeroy", name="Remaining Balance",
                        line=dict(color=COLORS["red"]),
                        fillcolor=f"rgba(239,68,68,0.2)",
                    ))
                    fig3.update_layout(
                        **PLOTLY_LAYOUT,
                        height=350,
                        xaxis_title="Months",
                        yaxis_title="Balance ($)",
                        yaxis=dict(gridcolor=COLORS["border"]),
                    )
                    st.plotly_chart(fig3, use_container_width=True)
                    total_months = len(schedule)
                    st.caption(
                        f"**{first_name}** — payoff in {total_months // 12}yr "
                        f"{total_months % 12}mo at ${total_pay:,.0f}/mo"
                    )
            else:
                st.info("Increase your payments to start reducing the balance.")
        else:
            st.markdown("### 💳 Debt Status")
            if not profile.debts:
                st.success("🎉 No debt recorded — keep it that way!")
            else:
                st.info("Build surplus through budgeting to start debt payoff projections.")

    # ── Savings Growth Projection ────────────────────────────────────────
    with chart4:
        st.markdown("### 📈 Savings Growth Projection")
        invest_amt = max(0, surplus * 0.3) if surplus > 0 else 100
        rate_map = {"low": 0.05, "medium": 0.07, "moderate": 0.07,
                    "high": 0.10, "very high": 0.12}
        annual_rate = rate_map.get(profile.risk_tolerance.lower(), 0.07)

        years = [1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30]
        balances = [compound_growth(invest_amt, annual_rate, y) for y in years]
        contributed = [invest_amt * 12 * y for y in years]

        fig4 = go.Figure()
        fig4.add_trace(go.Bar(
            x=[f"{y}yr" for y in years],
            y=contributed,
            name="Contributed",
            marker_color=COLORS["text_muted"],
        ))
        fig4.add_trace(go.Bar(
            x=[f"{y}yr" for y in years],
            y=[b - c for b, c in zip(balances, contributed)],
            name="Growth",
            marker_color=COLORS["green"],
        ))
        fig4.update_layout(
            **PLOTLY_LAYOUT,
            barmode="stack",
            height=350,
            yaxis_title="Balance ($)",
            yaxis=dict(gridcolor=COLORS["border"]),
        )
        st.plotly_chart(fig4, use_container_width=True)
        st.caption(f"${invest_amt:,.0f}/mo at ~{annual_rate*100:.0f}% → **${balances[-1]:,.0f}** in 30yr")

else:
    # No financial data yet
    st.markdown("---")
    _, ctr, _ = st.columns([1, 2, 1])
    with ctr:
        st.markdown(f"""
        <div style="text-align: center; padding: 3rem 0; color: {COLORS['text_muted']};">
            <div style="font-size: 3rem; margin-bottom: 1rem;">📊</div>
            <h3>Your dashboard will fill up as you share your financial info</h3>
            <p>Start chatting with an AI coach to see your metrics, charts, and projections here.</p>
        </div>
        """, unsafe_allow_html=True)
        st.page_link("pages/2_Chat.py", label="💬 Start Chatting →", use_container_width=True)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  BOTTOM SECTION — Quick Actions & Goals
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
st.markdown("---")
st.markdown("### 🚀 Quick Actions")

qa1, qa2, qa3 = st.columns(3)
with qa1:
    st.markdown(f"""
    <div class="agent-card" style="border-left: 3px solid {COLORS['gold']};">
        <span class="agent-icon">💬</span>
        <h4 style="color: {COLORS['gold']} !important;">Chat with a Coach</h4>
        <p>Get personalized advice from specialist AI agents</p>
    </div>
    """, unsafe_allow_html=True)
    st.page_link("pages/2_Chat.py", label="Open Chat →", use_container_width=True)

with qa2:
    st.markdown(f"""
    <div class="agent-card" style="border-left: 3px solid {COLORS['blue']};">
        <span class="agent-icon">🎓</span>
        <h4 style="color: {COLORS['blue']} !important;">Kids Corner</h4>
        <p>Video lessons and quizzes for financial literacy</p>
    </div>
    """, unsafe_allow_html=True)
    completed_vids = len(st.session_state.get("completed_videos", set()))
    if completed_vids > 0:
        st.caption(f"✅ {completed_vids} lessons completed")
    st.page_link("pages/3_Kids_Education.py", label="Learn →", use_container_width=True)

with qa3:
    st.markdown(f"""
    <div class="agent-card" style="border-left: 3px solid {COLORS['green']};">
        <span class="agent-icon">🎯</span>
        <h4 style="color: {COLORS['green']} !important;">My Goals</h4>
        <p>Track progress on your financial goals</p>
    </div>
    """, unsafe_allow_html=True)
    if profile.goals:
        for g in profile.goals[:3]:
            st.caption(f"• {g}")
    st.page_link("pages/4_My_Plan.py", label="View Plan →", use_container_width=True)

# ── Financial Health Indicators ──────────────────────────────────────────
if profile.completed_assessment:
    st.markdown("---")
    st.markdown("### 🏥 Financial Health")

    h1, h2, h3, h4 = st.columns(4)
    with h1:
        st.markdown("**Emergency Fund**")
        ef = emergency_fund_months(
            profile.emergency_fund or profile.savings_balance, expenses
        )
        st.progress(min(ef / 6, 1.0))
        st.caption(f"{ef:.1f} / 6 months")

    with h2:
        st.markdown("**Savings Rate**")
        st.progress(min(sr / 20, 1.0))
        st.caption(f"{sr:.0f}% / 20% target")

    with h3:
        st.markdown("**Debt-to-Income**")
        dti = profile.debt_to_income_ratio()
        st.progress(min(dti / 50, 1.0))
        color = "🟢" if dti < 20 else "🟡" if dti < 36 else "🔴"
        st.caption(f"{dti:.0f}% {color}")

    with h4:
        st.markdown("**Credit Score**")
        if profile.credit_score:
            st.progress(min(profile.credit_score / 850, 1.0))
            label = ("Excellent ✅" if profile.credit_score >= 750
                     else "Good 👍" if profile.credit_score >= 670
                     else "Fair ⚠️" if profile.credit_score >= 580
                     else "Needs Work 🔧")
            st.caption(f"{profile.credit_score} — {label}")
        else:
            st.caption("Not provided")

st.markdown("---")
st.caption("Last updated: this session • Dashboard auto-updates as you chat with agents")

FILE: pages/2_Chat.py  (411 lines)
